questions.specification:
The diagram shows two point masses: mass $A = 2m$ and mass $B = m$. Point $X$ lies between them, at a distance $2d$ from mass $A$ and $\sqrt{2} d$ from mass B.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/657f93d0-0be9-4b22-8574-3b2efb2aaf74)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic D - Fields > D.1 Gravitational fields > D.1.1 Laws and properties of gravitational fields
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D.1.1 Laws and properties of gravitational fields
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts involve gravitational field strength from point masses, superposition/zero-field condition, inverse-square dependence on distance, and qualitative stability of equilibrium at a point between masses—core laws/properties of gravitational fields.

parts.content: State the condition for the net gravitational field at point $X$ to be zero.
parts.markscheme:
The net gravitational field is zero when the fields from both masses are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction:

 $g_A = g_B$ 

<marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Understand gravitational field vectors
steps.content: In **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**, we define the gravitational field strength $g$ as a vector quantity. This means that at any point in space, the field has both a specific magnitude and a specific direction. When multiple masses are present, the net gravitational field is the vector sum of the individual fields produced by each mass.

steps.title: Identify field directions
steps.content:
Gravitational fields always point toward the mass creating them. 

At point $X$:
- The field due to mass $A$ ($g_A$) is directed toward the left (toward $A$).
- The field due to mass $B$ ($g_B$) is directed toward the right (toward $B$).

Because these two vectors point in exactly opposite directions along the line connecting the masses, they have the potential to cancel each other out.

steps.title: Apply the balance condition
steps.content:
For the net gravitational field at point $X$ to be zero, the individual field vectors must perfectly balance. This occurs when the fields from both masses are **equal in magnitude** and **opposite in direction**.

Since we already established they are opposite in direction, the mathematical condition is simply that their magnitudes must be equal:

$$g_A = g_B$$

highlights.text: fields from both masses are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction

steps.title: Recall the field formula
steps.content:
To find the net gravitational field, we first need to know how to calculate the gravitational field strength ($g$) from a point mass. According to the **Fields** section of your data booklet, the formula is:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^{2}}$$

Where $G$ is the gravitational constant, $M$ is the mass, and $r$ is the distance from the mass to the point in question. This concept is covered in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**.

steps.title: Analyze the field directions
steps.content:
Gravitational field strength is a vector quantity that always points towards the mass creating the field. 

At point $X$:
1. The field due to mass $A$ ($g_A$) points toward mass $A$ (to the left).
2. The field due to mass $B$ ($g_B$) points toward mass $B$ (to the right).

Since point $X$ lies between the two masses, these two fields act in exactly opposite directions.

steps.title: Apply Algebraic Field Equality
steps.content:
For the net (total) gravitational field at point $X$ to be zero, the two opposing fields must cancel each other out perfectly. This happens when their magnitudes are exactly equal.

The condition for the net field to be zero is:

$$g_A = g_B$$

Using the formula from the data booklet, we can express this equality as:

$$G\frac{M_A}{r_A^2} = G\frac{M_B}{r_B^2}$$

highlights.text: State the condition for the net gravitational field at point $X$ to be zero.

steps.title: Define the Test Mass
steps.content: To find where the net gravitational field is zero using the **Force Equilibrium Approach**, we imagine placing a small unit test mass ($m_{test}$) at point $X$. This question relates to **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**, where we study how masses exert forces on one another through fields.

highlights.text: net gravitational field at point X to be zero

steps.title: Analyze Force Equilibrium
steps.content:
According to Newton’s law of gravitation, both mass $A$ and mass $B$ will exert an attractive force on our test mass at $X$. 

$$\begin{aligned} F &= G\frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2} \end{aligned}$$

For the net gravitational field (and thus the net force) at point $X$ to be zero, the gravitational pull from mass $A$ must be perfectly balanced by the pull from mass $B$.

steps.title: State the Final Condition
steps.content:
Since gravitational field strength $g$ is defined as the force per unit mass ($g = \frac{F}{m}$), the condition for a net field of zero is that the magnitudes of the individual fields from $A$ and $B$ must be equal, while their directions are opposite.

$$g_A = g_B$$

parts.content: Show that the magnitudes of the gravitational fields due to each mass at point $X$ are equal.
parts.markscheme:
$g_A = \dfrac{G \cdot 2m}{(2d)^2} = \dfrac{2Gm}{4d^2} = \dfrac{Gm}{2d^2}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$g_B = \dfrac{G \cdot m}{(\sqrt{2} d)^2} = \dfrac{Gm}{2d^2}$

So: $g_A = g_B = \dfrac{Gm}{2d^2}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: If $m = 5.0 \times 10^{24} \ \text{kg}$ and $d = 1.0 \times 10^8 \ \text{m}$, calculate the magnitude of the gravitational field at $X$ due to mass $A$.
parts.markscheme:
Use 

$g = \dfrac{G \cdot 2m}{(2d)^2}$

$= \dfrac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 2 \cdot 5.0 \times 10^{24}}{(2.0 \times 10^8)^2}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$= \dfrac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{25}}{4.0 \times 10^{16}}$

$= \dfrac{6.67 \times 10^{14}}{4.0 \times 10^{16}} = 1.67 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{N kg}^{-1}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational field strength at point $X$, we use the formula from the **Fields** section of your data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

Where $G$ is the gravitational constant, $M$ is the mass creating the field, and $r$ is the distance from the center of that mass to the point in question.

steps.title: Determine mass and distance
steps.content:
From the question, we are calculating the field due to **mass A**. 
- The mass of A is given as $2m$. 
- The distance from mass A to point $X$ is given as $2d$.

Substituting the provided values for $m$ and $d$:
- $M = 2 \times (5.0 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg})$
- $r = 2 \times (1.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m})$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now we substitute all values into the formula, including the constant $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{kg}^{-2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} g &= \frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times (2 \times 5.0 \times 10^{24})}{(2 \times 1.0 \times 10^8)^2} \\ g &= \frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.0 \times 10^{25}}{4.0 \times 10^{16}} \end{aligned}$$

You would earn **1 mark** for showing this substitution correctly.

gdc.instructions: Use the fraction template and scientific notation.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the gravitational field.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the final calculation:

$$g = 1.67 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{N kg}^{-1}$$

This final value earns the **second mark**. Remember to include the correct units for gravitational field strength ($\text{N kg}^{-1}$ or $\text{m s}^{-2}$).

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational field strength at point $X$ due to mass $A$, we use the formula for gravitational field strength from the data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

This formula is found in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields** and describes how the field depends on the source mass $M$ and the distance $r$ from that mass.

steps.title: Perform algebraic reduction
steps.content:
Before plugging in numbers, let's simplify the expression using the variables given in the question. 

We know that for mass $A$:
- The mass $M = 2m$
- The distance $r = 2d$

Substituting these into our formula:

$$\begin{aligned} g &= G\frac{(2m)}{(2d)^2} \\ g &= \frac{2Gm}{4d^2} \\ g &= \frac{Gm}{2d^2} \end{aligned}$$

This algebraic reduction makes the final calculation much simpler!

steps.title: Substitute numerical values
steps.content:
Now we substitute the values for $G$, $m$, and $d$ into our reduced equation:

- $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^2 \text{kg}^{-2}$
- $m = 5.0 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$
- $d = 1.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ m}$

$$g = \frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (5.0 \times 10^{24})}{2 \times (1.0 \times 10^8)^2}$$

steps.title: Calculate using a GDC
steps.content: Use your calculator to solve the expression. Be careful with parentheses in the denominator to ensure the squaring and the multiplication by 2 are handled correctly.

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation into the main run-matrix screen. Use brackets to group the denominator.

ti84.instructions: Enter the numerator and denominator carefully. Use the [EE] key for powers of 10.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of gravitational field g

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The calculation gives:

$$g = 1.6675 \times 10^{-2} \text{ N kg}^{-1}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision of $G$ and our inputs):

$$g = 1.67 \times 10^{-2} \text{ N kg}^{-1}$$

According to the markscheme, the first mark is for correctly substituting the values into the formula, and the second mark is for the final correct answer with units.

parts.content: If a test mass were placed at point $X$, describe qualitatively what would happen if it were slightly displaced toward mass A.
parts.markscheme:
- The gravitational field becomes stronger from mass A as the test mass moves closer to it <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Therefore, the test mass is pulled further toward $A \rightarrow$ unstable equilibrium <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the field relationship
steps.content:
To understand how the test mass will behave, we first need to look at the formula for gravitational field strength ($g$). This is found in the **Fields** section of your data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

This equation shows us an **inverse square law**: the field strength is inversely proportional to the square of the distance ($r^2$). In other words, as you get closer to a mass, its gravitational pull becomes significantly stronger.

steps.title: Analyze the pull from A
steps.content:
When the test mass is **slightly displaced toward mass A**, the distance between the test mass and mass A decreases. 

According to the inverse square law, as the distance $r$ decreases, the gravitational field strength from mass A increases. This means mass A will exert a stronger pull on the test mass than it did at point X.

steps.title: Analyze the pull from B
steps.content:
At the same time, moving toward mass A means the test mass is moving further away from mass B. 

As the distance from mass B increases, the gravitational field strength from mass B decreases. Therefore, the pull from mass B becomes weaker.

steps.title: Determine the net effect
steps.content:
Initially, at point X, the pulls from A and B were balanced. Now, we have:
1. A **stronger** pull toward mass A.
2. A **weaker** pull toward mass B.

This creates a net (resultant) gravitational force directed toward mass A. The test mass will accelerate further toward A, moving even further away from its starting point.

steps.title: Conclusion: Unstable Equilibrium
steps.content:
Because a small displacement leads to a force that pushes the mass even further away from point X, we describe this as an **unstable equilibrium**.

In summary:
- The gravitational field from mass A becomes stronger as the mass moves closer.
- The test mass is pulled further toward A, confirming the equilibrium is unstable.

steps.title: Identify point X
steps.content:
In gravitational field theory, point $X$ represents a location where the gravitational fields of mass $A$ and mass $B$ cancel each other out. From a potential perspective, this point is a **local maximum** (a "peak") on a gravitational potential graph between the two masses.

While gravitational potential $V$ is always negative, point $X$ is the location where the potential is at its "highest" (closest to zero) before it drops sharply into the "potential wells" created by each mass.

steps.title: Analyze displacement toward A
steps.content:
When the test mass is slightly **displaced toward mass A**, the distance to mass $A$ decreases. 

The gravitational potential $V$ due to a point mass is given by the formula (found in **Topic D.1.3**):

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

As the distance $r$ to mass $A$ decreases, the potential $V_A$ becomes more negative (lower). This means the test mass is moving "downhill" from the local maximum at point $X$ toward the deeper potential well of mass $A$.

steps.title: Resulting motion and acceleration
steps.content:
Because objects naturally accelerate toward regions of lower gravitational potential, the test mass will experience a net force toward mass $A$. 

As it moves closer to $A$, the potential continues to drop even faster, and the gravitational field (the gradient of the potential) becomes even stronger. This causes the test mass to accelerate further away from its original position.

steps.title: Classify the equilibrium
steps.content:
Because a small displacement from point $X$ results in a force that pulls the mass further away from $X$ rather than back toward it, we describe point $X$ as a point of **unstable equilibrium**.

In summary, the test mass will be pulled further toward mass $A$ and will not return to point $X$.

questions.specification: A planet has mass $4.00 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$ and radius $5.00 \times 10^6\ \text{m}$. A $1000\ \text{kg}$ spacecraft is launched vertically from the surface to a point at $2.00 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$ from the planet’s centre.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic D - Fields > D.1 Gravitational fields > D.1.3 Gravitational potential energy and potential (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D.1.3 Gravitational potential energy and potential (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (b) require calculating gravitational potential energy at two radii and the work done moving between them (ΔGPE), which is directly gravitational potential energy/potential.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic D - Fields > D.1 Gravitational fields > D.1.4 Energetics of orbits and escape velocity (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D.1.4 Energetics of orbits and escape velocity (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (c) and (d) ask for escape speed and whether the calculated work is sufficient for escape, matching energetics of escape velocity.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational potential energy of the spacecraft at the surface and at the final point.
parts.markscheme:
- $E_p(\text{surface}) = -\frac{GMm}{R} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 4.00 \times 10^{24} \cdot 1000}{5.00 \times 10^6} = -5.34 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$
- $E_p(\text{final}) = -\frac{GMm}{r} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 4.00 \times 10^{24} \cdot 1000}{2.00 \times 10^7} = -1.33 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$ 
- Correct $E_p(\text{surface})$ calculation and value <marks>1 mark </marks>
- Correct $E_p(\text{final})$ calculation and value <marks>1 mark </marks>

parts.content: Determine the work required to move the spacecraft from the surface to the final point.
parts.markscheme:
- $W = \Delta E_p = -1.33 \times 10^{10} - (-5.34 \times 10^{10}) = 4.01 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$
- Correct formula <marks>1 mark </marks>
- Correct answer <marks>1 mark </marks>

parts.content: Calculate the escape speed from the planet’s surface.
parts.markscheme:
- $v_{\text{esc}} = \sqrt{\frac{2GM}{R}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 4.00 \times 10^{24}}{5.00 \times 10^6}} = 1.03 \times 10^4\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ 
- Correct formula <marks>1 mark </marks>
- Final answer <marks>1 mark </marks>

parts.content: Explain whether the work calculated in Part 2 would be enough to allow the spacecraft to escape.
parts.markscheme: No, because the spacecraft still retains a negative total energy at the final point—it hasn’t reached infinity, where $E = 0$ is required to escape. <marks>1 mark </marks>

questions.specification: The Moon orbits the Earth at an average distance of $3.84 \times 10^8\ \text{m}$. The mass of the Earth is $5.97 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$ and $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{N m}^2 \text{kg}^{-2}$.

parts.content: State the direction of the gravitational field produced by the Earth.
parts.markscheme: Towards the centre of the Earth / radially inward. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Visualize the field lines
steps.content: To determine the direction, we use the **Field Line Geometry** approach. Imagine the Earth as a spherical mass in space. Gravitational field lines are a visual tool used to represent the direction of the gravitational force that would act on a small test mass placed in the field.

steps.title: Analyze the geometry
steps.content: Since gravity is an attractive force, it pulls objects toward the massive body. For a spherical object like the Earth, this pull is directed towards its center from all directions. Visually, the field lines look like the spokes of a wheel, but pointing inward rather than outward.

steps.title: State the direction
steps.content:
Based on this geometry, the field lines point **radially inward**. This is the standard way to describe fields around spherical masses in **Topic D.1 Gravitational Fields**.

Answer: **Towards the centre of the Earth** (or **radially inward**).

steps.title: Define field direction
steps.content: To find the direction of a gravitational field, we use the **Force on Test Mass** concept. By definition, the direction of the gravitational field strength $g$ at any point is the same as the direction of the gravitational force $F$ that would act on a small test mass placed at that point.

steps.title: Analyze the force
steps.content: Gravity is always an attractive force. If we place a small test mass anywhere in the Moon's orbit or near the Earth, the Earth pulls that mass directly towards itself. Therefore, the force on the test mass points towards the center of the Earth.

steps.title: State the direction
steps.content: Since the force is directed towards the center, the gravitational field is also directed **towards the centre of the Earth** (or **radially inward**). Note that the numerical values given in the question are not needed for this part, as it simply asks for the direction.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational field strength at the Moon’s orbit due to the Earth.
parts.markscheme:
- $g = \frac{GM}{r^2} = \frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 5.97 \times 10^{24}}{(3.84 \times 10^8)^2} = 0.00270\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$
	- Correct formula and substitution <marks> 1 mark </marks> 
	- Correct final answer with unit <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Select the formula
steps.content: To calculate the gravitational field strength ($g$) at a distance $r$ from the center of a planet, we use the formula from the **Fields** section of the data booklet. Here, $M$ is the mass of the Earth causing the field.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
Identify the values for the gravitational constant $G$, the mass of the Earth $M$, and the orbital radius $r$ from the question and data booklet.

$$\begin{aligned} G &= 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{N m}^2 \text{kg}^{-2} \\ M &= 5.97 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg} \\ r &= 3.84 \times 10^8\ \text{m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute values
steps.content:
Substitute these values directly into the equation. Remember to square the distance $r$ in the denominator.

$$g = \frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (5.97 \times 10^{24})}{(3.84 \times 10^8)^2}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the fraction carefully using parentheses for scientific notation.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly.

calculator.description: Calculate gravitational field strength

steps.title: Calculate final answer
steps.content:
Compute the result using your calculator.

$$g \approx 0.0027004...\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$$ 

Rounding to 3 significant figures gives:

$$g = 0.00270\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$$

parts.content: Explain why the Moon remains in orbit despite this weak field.
parts.markscheme: The gravitational field, though weak, provides the centripetal force needed to maintain the Moon’s circular orbit. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Analyze the motion requirements
steps.content:
To maintain a circular orbit, the Moon requires a resultant force acting towards the center of the circle (the Earth). This resultant force is known as the **centripetal force**. 

Without this force, Newton's First Law states that the Moon would continue moving in a straight line at a constant velocity.

steps.title: Identify the acting force
steps.content:
The question refers to the Earth's gravitational field. Even though the question describes it as a "weak field", it is the only significant force acting on the Moon. 

From the data booklet in the **Fields** section, we know the gravitational force is attractive:

$$F = G\frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}$$

steps.title: Connect gravity to centripetal force
steps.content:
The explanation lies in identifying that the gravitational force **provides** the necessary centripetal force. 

This force acts perpendicular to the Moon's velocity, which constantly changes the direction of the Moon's motion without changing its speed, keeping it in a stable circular path rather than flying off into space.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The gravitational force provides the centripetal force required to maintain the Moon's circular orbit.

Note: This relates to the circular motion equation from the **Mechanics** section:

$$a = \frac{v^2}{r}$$

questions.specification: A satellite is in circular orbit $2.00 \times 10^6\ \text{m}$ above the surface of Earth. The radius of Earth is $6.37 \times 10^6\ \text{m}$, and Earth’s mass is $5.97 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$.

parts.content: Calculate the distance from the centre of the Earth to the satellite.
parts.markscheme:
Addition of Earth's radius and altitude: $6.37 \times 10^6 + 2.00 \times 10^6 = 8.37 \times 10^6\ \text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To solve this, we need to extract two key pieces of information from the question: the height of the satellite above the Earth's surface (altitude) and the radius of the Earth itself.

- Altitude ($h$) = $2.00 \times 10^6\ \text{m}$
- Earth's radius ($R_{E}$) = $6.37 \times 10^6\ \text{m}$

steps.title: Define the orbital radius
steps.content:
In gravitational problems (Topic D.1), the distance $r$ always represents the distance from the **center** of one mass to the center of the other. For a satellite, this is the sum of the planet's radius and the satellite's altitude.

$$\text{Total distance } (r) = \text{Radius } (R_{E}) + \text{Altitude } (h)$$

This is a crucial step because gravitational formulas like $g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$ require the distance from the center, not just the height above the ground.

steps.title: Calculate the total distance
steps.content:
Now, we simply add the two values together to find the total distance from the center of the Earth:

$$\begin{aligned} r &= 6.37 \times 10^6\ \text{m} + 2.00 \times 10^6\ \text{m} \\ r &= 8.37 \times 10^6\ \text{m} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns the mark by showing the correct addition of the radius and altitude.

gdc.instructions: Input 6.37 * 10^6 + 2.00 * 10^6 into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 6.37E6 + 2.00E6 (using the [2nd] [,] keys for the 'E' exponent function).

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the radius and altitude.

parts.content: Determine the gravitational field strength at the satellite’s altitude.
parts.markscheme:
Substitution into $g = \frac{GM}{r^2} = \frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 5.97 \times 10^{24}}{(8.37 \times 10^6)^2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$g = 5.68\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

methods.label: Direct Calculation with Gravitational Field Formula

steps.title: Calculate the orbital radius
steps.content:
To find the gravitational field strength at the satellite's position, we first need the total distance $r$ from the center of the Earth. This is the sum of the Earth's radius and the satellite's altitude above the surface.

$$\begin{aligned} r &= \text{radius of Earth} + \text{altitude} \\ r &= 6.37 \times 10^6\text{ m} + 2.00 \times 10^6\text{ m} \\ r &= 8.37 \times 10^6\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the field formula
steps.content:
From the **Fields** section of your data booklet, we can use the formula for gravitational field strength $g$ at a distance $r$ from a mass $M$. We also need the gravitational constant $G$ from the constants page.

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

Where:
- $$G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\text{ N m}^{2}\text{ kg}^{-2}$$
- $$M = 5.97 \times 10^{24}\text{ kg}$$ (Mass of Earth)

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the formula to calculate the field strength at the satellite's altitude. 

$$\begin{aligned} g &= \frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 5.97 \times 10^{24}}{(8.37 \times 10^6)^2} \\ g &\approx 5.68\text{ N kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation would earn you the first mark for substitution and the second mark for the correct final answer.

steps.title: Find total orbital radius
steps.content:
To find the gravitational field strength at the satellite's position, we first need to determine its total distance $r$ from the center of the Earth. This is the sum of the Earth's radius and the satellite's altitude above the surface.

$$\begin{aligned} r &= \text{Radius of Earth} + \text{altitude} \\ r &= 6.37 \times 10^6\text{ m} + 2.00 \times 10^6\text{ m} \\ r &= 8.37 \times 10^6\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Use inverse square law
steps.content:
From the data booklet, the formula for gravitational field strength is:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

Since $G$ and $M$ are constants, we can see that $g$ is inversely proportional to the square of the distance ($g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$). 

We can compare the field strength at the satellite's orbit ($g$) to the field strength at the Earth's surface ($g_s = 9.81\text{ N kg}^{-1}$):

$$\frac{g}{g_s} = \frac{R^2}{r^2} \implies g = g_s \left( \frac{R}{r} \right)^2$$

steps.title: Calculate the field strength
steps.content:
Now we substitute the values into our ratio equation:

$$g = 9.81 \times \left( \frac{6.37 \times 10^6}{8.37 \times 10^6} \right)^2$$

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Using the calculation from the previous step:

$$g \approx 5.68\text{ N kg}^{-1}$$

This value represents the acceleration due to gravity experienced by the satellite at its specific altitude. This aligns with the markscheme's value obtained using the direct formula.

parts.content: Calculate the orbital speed required for the satellite to stay in circular orbit.
parts.markscheme:
Substitution into $v = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}}$ or $v = \sqrt{gr}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$v = 6.90 \times 10^3\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Calculate the total orbital radius
steps.content:
For a satellite in orbit, the radius $r$ in our equations represents the distance from the **center** of the planet. 

We calculate this by adding the Earth's radius to the altitude above the surface:

$$\begin{aligned} r &= \text{radius of Earth} + \text{altitude} \\ r &= (6.37 \times 10^6\text{ m}) + (2.00 \times 10^6\text{ m}) \\ r &= 8.37 \times 10^6\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Equate the physical forces
steps.content:
In circular orbit, the gravitational force ($F_g$) between the Earth and the satellite provides the required centripetal force ($F_c$). 

From the **Fields** and **Mechanics** sections of your data booklet:

$$F_g = G \frac{M m}{r^2} \quad \text{and} \quad F_c = \frac{m v^2}{r}$$

Where:
- $M$ is the mass of Earth
- $m$ is the mass of the satellite
- $v$ is the orbital speed

steps.title: Derive the speed formula
steps.content:
We set the two forces equal to each other and solve for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} G \frac{M m}{r^2} &= \frac{m v^2}{r} \\ \frac{G M}{r} &= v^2 \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}} \end{aligned}$$

Note that the mass of the satellite ($m$) cancels out, meaning the required speed depends only on the mass of the planet and the orbital radius. This is a key concept in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**.

steps.title: Calculate the final speed
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values from the question and the constants from the data booklet:
- $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{kg}^{-2}$
- $M = 5.97 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$
- $r = 8.37 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (5.97 \times 10^{24})}{8.37 \times 10^6}}$$

$$v \approx 6.90 \times 10^3 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

2. Use the square root function.
3. Input the fraction ((6.67e-11 * 5.97e24) / 8.37e6).
4. Evaluate.

1. Press [2nd] [x^2] (square root symbol).
2. Enter the numerator: (6.67 * 10^-11 * 5.97 * 10^24).
3. Press [/] and enter the denominator: (8.37 * 10^6).
4. Close the parentheses and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the orbital speed using the formula derived.

steps.title: Determine the orbital radius
steps.content:
To calculate the speed, we first need the total orbital radius $r$, which is the distance from the center of Earth to the satellite. This is the sum of Earth's radius and the altitude of the satellite.

$$\begin{aligned} r &= R_{Earth} + \text{altitude} \\ r &= 6.37 \times 10^6\text{ m} + 2.00 \times 10^6\text{ m} \\ r &= 8.37 \times 10^6\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Add Earth's radius and the altitude to find the total orbital radius.

steps.title: Calculate local field strength
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the gravitational field strength $g$ at this specific orbital radius using the formula from the **Fields** section of the data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\begin{aligned} g &= (6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \frac{5.97 \times 10^{24}}{(8.37 \times 10^6)^2} \\ g &\approx 5.684 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational field strength at the orbital radius.

steps.title: Relate field to speed
steps.content:
In a circular orbit, the gravitational field strength providing the acceleration is the centripetal acceleration. We use the formula from the **Mechanics** section:

$$a = \frac{v^2}{r}$$

Equating the two, we have $g = \frac{v^2}{r}$, which we can rearrange to solve for the orbital speed $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{g \cdot r}$$

steps.title: Calculate orbital speed
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our values for $g$ and $r$ into the rearranged equation to find the final speed:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{5.684 \times (8.37 \times 10^6)} \\ v &\approx 6897.4 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $v = 6.90 \times 10^3 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. 

This substitution and calculation earns both marks (M1 for the correct substitution and A1 for the final answer).

calculator.description: Calculate the final orbital speed from g and r.

parts.content: Explain why the gravitational field strength decreases with altitude.
parts.markscheme:
Recognition that gravitational field strength follows an inverse square law ($g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$), so it decreases as the distance $r$ from the centre of the Earth increases <marks>R1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To explain why the gravitational field strength changes, we start with the formula from the **Fields** section of the IB Physics data booklet:

$$g = \frac{GM}{r^2}$$

In this equation:
- $G$ is the universal gravitational constant.
- $M$ is the mass of the Earth.
- $r$ is the distance from the center of the Earth to the point of interest.

steps.title: Relate altitude to distance
steps.content:
The distance $r$ represents the total distance from the Earth's center. This is the sum of the Earth's radius and the altitude above the surface:

$$r = R_{Earth} + \text{altitude}$$

As the satellite's altitude increases, the total distance $r$ from the center of the Earth also increases.

steps.title: Apply inverse square law
steps.content:
Since $G$ and $M$ are constants, the formula shows that $g$ is inversely proportional to the square of the distance $r$:

$$g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$$

This is known as the **inverse square law**. Because $r$ is in the denominator, an increase in $r$ (due to higher altitude) results in a decrease in the gravitational field strength $g$.

steps.title: Identify the field equation
steps.content:
To understand how gravity changes with height, we look at the formula for gravitational field strength ($g$) found in the **Fields** section of your data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

This equation shows that the field strength follows an **inverse square law**, which means $g$ is proportional to the inverse of the square of the distance ($g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$).

steps.title: Relate altitude to distance
steps.content:
In this context, $r$ represents the total distance from the **center of the Earth**. 

When a satellite's **altitude** increases, it is moving further away from the surface. This causes the total distance $r$ from the Earth's center to increase as well.

steps.title: Conclude the relationship
steps.content:
Because the relationship is an inverse square law, any increase in the distance ($r$) results in a decrease in the gravitational field strength ($g$). 

Therefore, as altitude increases, the gravitational field strength decreases. 

To earn the mark in an IB exam, you must explicitly mention the **inverse square law** ($g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$) and state that $r$ increases with altitude.

steps.title: Visualize field lines
steps.content:
To understand gravitational field strength ($g$), we can visualize it using **field lines**. For a spherical body like Earth, these lines point radially inward toward the center. 

You can imagine these lines originating from infinity and bunching together as they get closer to the Earth's surface.

steps.title: Link density to strength
steps.content:
In physics, the **density** of these field lines (how close together they are) represents the magnitude of the field strength. 

*   **High density** (lines close together) = Strong gravitational field.
*   **Low density** (lines far apart) = Weak gravitational field.

steps.title: Explain spreading with altitude
steps.content:
As you move to a higher **altitude**, the distance $r$ from the center of the Earth increases. Because the field lines spread out radially as they move away from the center, they must cover a larger and larger volume of space.

Because the same number of field lines is spread over a larger area at higher altitudes, the **field line density decreases**. This tells us visually that the field strength must be getting weaker.

highlights.text: Explain why the gravitational field strength decreases with altitude.

steps.title: Relate to the formula
steps.content:
This visual "spreading out" is mathematically expressed by the **inverse square law** found in the **Fields** section of your data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

This formula shows that $g$ is inversely proportional to the square of the distance ($g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$). As the distance $r$ from the center of the Earth increases with altitude, the gravitational field strength $g$ decreases. To earn the mark, you must mention that $g$ follows this inverse square law relationship.

A spacecraft of mass $1500\ \text{kg}$ is to be launched from the surface of planet X into a circular orbit at an altitude of $1000\ \text{km}$ above its surface. The mass of Earth is $5.97 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$ and its radius is $6.37 \times 10^6\ \text{m}$.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational field strength at the surface of planet X.
parts.markscheme:
$g_X = \frac{G(2M_E)}{(1.5R_E)^2} = \frac{2}{2.25} \times g_E$ <marks>1</marks>
$g_X = \frac{2}{2.25} \times 9.81 = 8.72\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ (accept $8.72\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$) <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational field strength at the surface of a planet, we use the formula from the **Fields** section of the IB Physics data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^{2}}$$

Where:
- $G$ is the gravitational constant ($6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{ kg}^{-2}$)
- $M$ is the mass of the planet
- $r$ is the radius of the planet

steps.title: Calculate Planet X's mass
steps.content:
The question states that Planet X has **twice the mass of Earth**. We calculate the absolute mass ($M_X$) using the provided mass of Earth ($M_E = 5.97 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$):

$$\begin{aligned} M_X &= 2 \times M_E \\ M_X &= 2 \times 5.97 \times 10^{24} \\ M_X &= 1.194 \times 10^{25} \text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * 5.97 * 10^24 into the calculator application.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 * 5.97E24 into the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of Planet X by doubling Earth's mass.

steps.title: Calculate Planet X's radius
steps.content:
The question states Planet X has a **radius 1.5 times that of Earth**. We calculate the absolute radius ($R_X$) using the provided radius of Earth ($R_E = 6.37 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}$):

$$\begin{aligned} R_X &= 1.5 \times R_E \\ R_X &= 1.5 \times 6.37 \times 10^{6} \\ R_X &= 9.555 \times 10^{6} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1.5 * 6.37 * 10^6 into the calculator application.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.5 * 6.37E6 into the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the radius of Planet X.

steps.title: Calculate field strength
steps.content:
Now, substitute the absolute values for $G$, $M_X$, and $R_X$ into the gravitational field strength formula:

$$\begin{aligned} g_X &= \frac{G M_X}{R_X^{2}} \\ g_X &= \frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (1.194 \times 10^{25})}{(9.555 \times 10^{6})^{2}} \\ g_X &\approx 8.72 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (6.67 * 10^-11 * 1.194 * 10^25) / (9.555 * 10^6)^2 into the calculator application.

ti84.instructions: Enter (6.67E-11 * 1.194E25) / (9.555E6)^2 into the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the final gravitational field strength $g_X$.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The gravitational field strength at the surface of Planet X is $8.72 \text{ m s}^{-2}$. 

Note that in the markscheme, the first mark is awarded for correctly identifying the ratio of the field strength (the $M/R^2$ relationship), and the second mark is for the final numerical answer.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
To solve this using ratios, we first look at the formula for gravitational field strength ($g$) from the **Fields** section of your data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

Since $G$ is a constant, we can see that the gravitational field strength is directly proportional to the mass ($M$) and inversely proportional to the square of the radius ($r$):

$$g \propto \frac{M}{r^2}$$

steps.title: Apply scaling factors
steps.content:
The question provides scaling factors for Planet X compared to Earth. Let's identify them:
- Mass ($M_X$) is **twice** the mass of Earth: $M_X = 2M_E$
- Radius ($r_X$) is **1.5 times** the radius of Earth: $r_X = 1.5R_E$

Substituting these into our proportionality relationship, we get:

$$g_X \propto \frac{2M_E}{(1.5R_E)^2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the ratio
steps.content:
Now we simplify the ratio of $g_X$ to $g_E$. 

$$\begin{aligned} g_X &= \frac{2}{1.5^2} \times g_E \\ g_X &= \frac{2}{2.25} \times g_E \end{aligned}$$

This shows that the gravity on Planet X is approximately $0.89$ times that of Earth's gravity.

steps.title: Find the final value
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply this ratio by Earth's gravitational field strength ($g_E = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$):

$$\begin{aligned} g_X &= \frac{2}{2.25} \times 9.81 \\ g_X &= 8.72\text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns both marks: one for the correct ratio setup and one for the final numerical answer.

parts.content: Determine the orbital radius of the spacecraft.
parts.markscheme:
$r = R_X + h = 1.5 \times (6.37 \times 10^6) + 1.0 \times 10^6$
$r = 9.555 \times 10^6 + 1.0 \times 10^6 = 1.0555 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$ <marks>1</marks>

methods.label: Sum of Planet Radius and Altitude

steps.title: Understand the orbital radius
steps.content:
In gravitational physics, the **orbital radius** ($r$) is the total distance from the **center** of the planet to the object in orbit. This is always the sum of the planet's actual radius ($R_X$) and the altitude ($h$) of the spacecraft above the surface.

$$r = R_X + h$$

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**.

steps.title: Calculate Planet X's radius
steps.content:
First, we need to find the radius of Planet X. The question states that its radius is 1.5 times that of Earth.

$$\begin{aligned} R_X &= 1.5 \times R_{\text{Earth}} \\ R_X &= 1.5 \times (6.37 \times 10^6 \text{ m}) \\ R_X &= 9.555 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the radius of Planet X by multiplying the Earth's radius by 1.5.

steps.title: Convert altitude to meters
steps.content:
Next, we look at the altitude ($h$). To add it to the radius, it must be in SI units (meters). 

$$\begin{aligned} h &= 1000 \text{ km} \\ h &= 1000 \times 10^3 \text{ m} \\ h &= 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine total orbital radius
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the planet's radius and the altitude to find the total orbital radius from the center.

$$\begin{aligned} r &= R_X + h \\ r &= 9.555 \times 10^6 \text{ m} + 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \\ r &= 1.0555 \times 10^7 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation ensures you account for the distance from the center, which is the value required for any subsequent orbital mechanics calculations (like finding orbital speed or period).

calculator.description: Sum the planet radius and the altitude to find the total orbital radius.

parts.content: Calculate the orbital speed required for circular motion.
parts.markscheme:
$v = \sqrt{\frac{GM_X}{r}} = \sqrt{\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2 \times 5.97 \times 10^{24}}{1.0555 \times 10^7}}$ <marks>1</marks>
$v = 8690\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ (accept $8686\ \text{m s}^{-1}$) <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the total mechanical energy of the spacecraft in orbit.
parts.markscheme:
$E_T = -\frac{GM_X m}{2r} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2 \times 5.97 \times 10^{24} \times 1500}{2 \times 1.0555 \times 10^7}$ <marks>1</marks>
$E_T = -5.66 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$ <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how the energy required to launch the spacecraft into this orbit compares with the same operation on Earth.
parts.markscheme:
More energy is required on planet X. <marks>1</marks>
Although the surface gravity is slightly lower, the gravitational potential well is significantly deeper due to the much larger mass ($M/R$ ratio is larger), requiring more work to reach orbit. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define the Potential Well
steps.content:
To compare the energy required, we look at the **gravitational potential** ($V$) at the surface. Gravitational potential represents the "depth" of a gravity well. It is defined by the formula:

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

where $M$ is the mass of the planet and $r$ is the distance from its center. The more negative the potential, the "deeper" the well, and the more energy is needed to move a mass out of it.

steps.title: Compare the $M/R$ Ratios
steps.content:
The depth of the potential well at the surface depends on the ratio of the planet's mass to its radius ($\frac{M}{R}$). Let's compare the ratios for Planet X and Earth using the given information:

- **Planet X:** $M_X = 2M_E$ and $R_X = 1.5R_E$
- **Earth:** $M_E$ and $R_E$

We can calculate the relative ratio for Planet X:

gdc.instructions: Divide 2 by 1.5.

ti84.instructions: Divide 2 by 1.5 to find the ratio.

desmos.instructions: Divide 2 by 1.5.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of Mass/Radius for Planet X relative to Earth.

steps.title: Analyze the Well Depth
steps.content:
Using our calculation:

$$\frac{M_X}{R_X} = \frac{2M_E}{1.5R_E} \approx 1.33 \times \frac{M_E}{R_E}$$

This means the ratio of mass to radius is **larger** for Planet X than for Earth. Even though Planet X is larger in size, its doubled mass makes the gravitational potential at its surface significantly lower (more negative) than Earth's.

steps.title: Evaluate Energy Requirement
steps.content:
To launch a spacecraft into orbit, work must be done against the gravitational field to move the mass from a deeper potential to a higher one. 

Because the potential well on Planet X is **deeper** (due to the larger $M/R$ ratio), more work (energy) is required to "climb" out of that well to reach the target orbit, despite the surface gravity ($g$) actually being slightly lower on Planet X.

highlights.text: More energy is required on planet X.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
In conclusion, **more energy is required on Planet X**. 

While the surface gravity $g$ (which depends on $M/R^2$) is slightly lower, the total energy required to reach orbit is determined by the change in potential, which is dominated by the **significantly deeper potential well** (larger $M/R$ ratio) of the more massive planet.

highlights.text: gravitational potential well is significantly deeper due to the much larger mass ($M/R$ ratio is larger)

steps.title: Define energy required
steps.content:
To find the energy required to launch a spacecraft into orbit, we look at the change in total energy ($\Delta E$). This is the difference between the energy in orbit and the energy at the surface:

$$\text{Work} = E_{\text{orbit}} - E_{\text{surface}}$$

On the surface, the spacecraft has only gravitational potential energy ($E_p = -\frac{GMm}{R}$). In a circular orbit, the total energy (kinetic + potential) is given by:

$$E_{\text{total}} = -\frac{GMm}{2r}$$

Both terms are proportional to the ratio $\frac{M}{r}$. We can use scaling factors for mass ($M$) and radius ($R$) to compare Planet X to Earth.

steps.title: Analyze the scaling factors
steps.content:
We are given that Planet X has **twice the mass** and **1.5 times the radius** of Earth. Let's look at how the magnitude of the gravitational energy scales by comparing the $\frac{M}{R}$ ratios:

$$\text{Scaling Factor} = \frac{M_X / R_X}{M_E / R_E} = \frac{2}{1.5}$$

Using a calculator, we find:

$$\frac{2}{1.5} \approx 1.33$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 2 by 1.5.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 divided by 1.5 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Input the fraction 2/1.5.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of mass to radius for Planet X relative to Earth.

steps.title: Compare energy requirements
steps.content:
Since the ratio $\frac{M}{R}$ is larger for Planet X ($1.33$ times that of Earth), the gravitational potential well is **deeper**. 

Even though the surface gravity ($g = \frac{GM}{R^2}$) might be slightly lower on Planet X, the total work required to move the spacecraft from the surface to the orbit depends on the potential difference. Because Planet X is much more massive, it requires more work to increase the spacecraft's potential energy and provide the necessary orbital kinetic energy.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
More energy is required to launch the spacecraft on Planet X than on Earth. 

This is because the significantly larger mass of Planet X results in a higher $\frac{M}{R}$ ratio, meaning the spacecraft must overcome a much stronger gravitational bond to reach its orbital destination.

- Equipotential surfaces are perpendicular to the field lines, and no work is done when moving along them. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

questions.specification: Two identical asteroids, each of mass $2.00 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kg}$, are initially at rest and separated by $4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$ in deep space.

parts.content: Calculate the initial gravitational potential energy of the system.
parts.markscheme:
Use of $E_p = -\frac{Gm_1m_2}{r}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Substitute values:
$$E_p = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times (2.00 \times 10^{12})^2}{4.00 \times 10^3}$$

$-6.67 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the required formula
steps.content:
To calculate the energy of the system, we need the formula for gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) between two point masses. 

This concept is explored in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**. In IB Physics, the potential energy for a pair of masses is given by:

$$E_p = -\frac{Gm_1m_2}{r}$$

Notice that the value is always negative because gravity is an attractive force, and we define the potential energy to be zero at an infinite distance.

steps.title: Extract known values
steps.content:
From the question and the **Physical Constants** section of your data booklet, we identify the following values:

- Mass of each asteroid: $m_1 = m_2 = 2.00 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kg}$
- Separation distance: $r = 4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$
- Gravitational constant: $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{N m}^{2} \text{kg}^{-2}$

steps.title: Calculate potential energy
steps.content:
Now, we perform the direct substitution into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times (2.00 \times 10^{12}) \times (2.00 \times 10^{12})}{4.00 \times 10^3} \\ E_p &= -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 4.00 \times 10^{24}}{4.00 \times 10^3} \\ E_p &= -6.67 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

In the exam, showing the substitution clearly earns you the first mark (**M1**).

gdc.instructions: Use the calculation menu to evaluate the formula.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen. Use the [EE] key for scientific notation.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential energy by substituting the mass and distance values.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
The initial gravitational potential energy of the system is:

$$E_p = -6.67 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$$

Ensure you include the negative sign and the correct unit (Joules) to secure the final mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To find the initial gravitational potential energy of the system, let's first list the values we know from the question:

*   Mass of each asteroid ($m_1$ and $m_2$): $2.00 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kg}$
*   Initial separation distance ($r$): $4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$
*   Gravitational constant ($G$): $6.67 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{N m}^2\ \text{kg}^{-2}$

This problem involves concepts from **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**, specifically focusing on how potential energy is stored in a field between two masses.

steps.title: Calculate gravitational potential
steps.content:
Using the **Gravitational Potential Approach**, we first calculate the gravitational potential ($V_g$) created by one asteroid at the location of the other. 

The formula for gravitational potential is:
$$V_g = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

Substituting our values:
$$V_g = -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (2.00 \times 10^{12})}{4.00 \times 10^3}$$
$$V_g = -0.03335\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate -(6.67E-11 * 2E12) / 4E3.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential V_g.

steps.title: Calculate system potential energy
steps.content:
Now, we find the total gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) of the system. This is the energy required to bring the second mass from infinity to its current position in the potential field created by the first mass.

We use the relationship:
$$E_p = m V_g$$

Substituting the mass of the second asteroid and the potential we just calculated:
$$E_p = (2.00 \times 10^{12}) \times (-0.03335)$$
$$E_p = -6.67 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2E12 * -0.03335.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the mass by the previously calculated value (Ans).

desmos.instructions: Perform the multiplication.

calculator.description: Multiply the mass by the potential to find potential energy.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The initial gravitational potential energy of the system is **$-6.67 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$**.

In an IB exam, you would earn marks as follows:
*   **M1**: For correctly using the energy formula or identifying the components of the potential approach.
*   **A1**: For the correct numerical answer with the negative sign and correct units.

parts.content: Determine the gravitational potential at the midpoint between the two asteroids.
parts.markscheme:
Use of $V = -\frac{GM}{r}$ for each mass OR sum of potentials <marks>M1</marks>

Substitute values:
$$V_{\text{mid}} = -\frac{Gm}{r/2} - \frac{Gm}{r/2} = -\frac{4Gm}{r}$$
$$V_{\text{mid}} = -\frac{4 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.00 \times 10^{12}}{4.00 \times 10^3}$$

$-0.133 \text{ OR } -1.33 \times 10^{-1}\ (\text{J kg}^{-1})$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the midpoint distance
steps.content:
To find the gravitational potential at the midpoint, we first need the distance from each asteroid to that center point. Since the total separation is $4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$, the distance $r$ to the midpoint is exactly half of that.

$$r_{\text{mid}} = \frac{4.00 \times 10^3}{2} = 2.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$$

steps.title: Recall the potential formula
steps.content:
Gravitational potential ($V$) is a scalar quantity that tells us the work done per unit mass. This is covered in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**. The formula for the potential created by a single point mass $M$ at a distance $r$ is:

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

Notice the negative sign! Gravitational potential is always negative (defined as zero at infinity).

steps.title: Calculate individual potential
steps.content:
Using the mass of one asteroid $M = 2.00 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kg}$ and our calculated distance $r = 2.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$, let's find the potential contribution from just one asteroid ($V_1$).

$$V_1 = -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (2.00 \times 10^{12})}{2.00 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_1 = -0.0667\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Input the fraction into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation directly: -(6.67e-11 * 2.00e12) / 2000

desmos.instructions: Type the formula using scientific notation.

calculator.description: Calculate the potential contribution from a single asteroid.

steps.title: Sum the potentials
steps.content:
Because potential is a scalar, the total potential at the midpoint is simply the algebraic sum of the potentials from both asteroids. Since the asteroids are identical and the distances are equal, we just double the individual potential:

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 = 2 \times V_1$$

$$V_{\text{total}} = 2 \times (-0.0667) = -0.1334\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the result from the previous step by 2.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by 2.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the calculated $V_1$ by 2.

calculator.description: Double the individual potential to find the total.

steps.title: Final result
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures, we get the total gravitational potential at the midpoint:

$$V_{\text{mid}} = -0.133\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$$

(Note: You could also express this as $-1.33 \times 10^{-1}\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$ in scientific notation.)

steps.title: Identify potential and superposition
steps.content:
To find the total gravitational potential at the midpoint, we use the principle of superposition. This states that the total potential ($V_{\text{total}}$) is the scalar sum of the potentials created by each individual asteroid. 

The general formula for gravitational potential is:

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

where $M$ is the mass of the asteroid and $r$ is the distance from its center to the point of interest.

highlights.text: Two identical asteroids, each of mass $2.00 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kg}$

steps.title: Derive the combined formula
steps.content:
Since both asteroids have the same mass ($M$) and the midpoint is exactly halfway between them, the distance from each asteroid to the midpoint is $r = \frac{d}{2}$, where $d$ is the total separation.

Applying the principle of superposition:

$$\begin{aligned} V_{\text{total}} &= V_1 + V_2 \\ V_{\text{total}} &= -\frac{GM}{\frac{d}{2}} - \frac{GM}{\frac{d}{2}} \\ V_{\text{total}} &= -\frac{2GM}{d} - \frac{2GM}{d} \\ V_{\text{total}} &= -\frac{4GM}{d} \end{aligned}$$

This combined algebraic formula allows us to calculate the result in one step.

steps.title: Substitute numerical values
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into our derived formula:
- $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{N m}^2 \text{kg}^{-2}$
- $M = 2.00 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kg}$
- $d = 4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$

$$\begin{aligned} V_{\text{total}} &= -\frac{4 \times (6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (2.00 \times 10^{12})}{4.00 \times 10^3} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the values into the calculation field.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total gravitational potential using the combined formula.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives:

$$V_{\text{total}} = -0.1334\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:

$$V_{\text{total}} = -0.133\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$$

Note that gravitational potential is always negative relative to infinity, indicating a bound system.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational field strength at the midpoint between the two asteroids.
parts.markscheme:
Recognition that gravitational fields cancel / sum to zero <marks>M1</marks>

Substitute values:
$$g = \frac{Gm}{(r/2)^2} - \frac{Gm}{(r/2)^2}$$

$0 \text{ OR } 0\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the midpoint conditions
steps.content:
In this problem, we are looking for the net gravitational field at the **midpoint** between two objects. Because the asteroids are described as **identical**, we know they have the same mass ($M = 2.00 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kg}$). 

At the midpoint, the distance from each asteroid is exactly half of the total separation ($r$):

$$\text{distance to each asteroid} = \frac{4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}}{2} = 2.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$$

steps.title: Identify field vector directions
steps.content:
Gravitational field strength ($g$) is a **vector quantity**. The field produced by a mass always points **towards** that mass. 

From the **Fields** section of your data booklet, the magnitude of the field is given by:

$$g = G \frac{M}{r^2}$$

At the midpoint:
1. The field from the left asteroid points to the left.
2. The field from the right asteroid points to the right.

steps.title: Apply symmetry for cancellation
steps.content:
Because the masses ($M$) are identical and the distances ($r$) to the midpoint are equal, the magnitudes of the two gravitational fields must be exactly the same.

Since the two field vectors have the **same magnitude** but point in **opposite directions**, they will perfectly cancel each other out when summed. 

$$\begin{aligned} g_{net} &= g_{left} - g_{right} \\ g_{net} &= \frac{GM}{(r/2)^2} - \frac{GM}{(r/2)^2} \\ g_{net} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the field magnitude: (6.67 * 10^-11 * 2 * 10^12) / 2000^2

ti84.instructions: Calculate the magnitude of one field: (6.67E-11 * 2E12) / (2000^2)

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula for field magnitude to see the value.

calculator.description: Verify that the magnitudes are equal using the provided values.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The net gravitational field strength at the midpoint is $0\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$.

In an IB exam, simply stating that the fields cancel or sum to zero due to symmetry is sufficient to earn the first mark (M1), while providing the final value of zero earns the second mark (A1).

steps.title: Determine the midpoint distance
steps.content:
To find the gravitational field at the midpoint, we first need the distance $r$ from the center of each asteroid to that point. The asteroids are separated by $4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$, so the distance to the midpoint is half of that:

$$r = \frac{4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}}{2} = 2.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$$

steps.title: Calculate individual field magnitude
steps.content:
Gravitational field strength $g$ is a vector quantity. From the data booklet, the magnitude of the field produced by a single mass $M$ at a distance $r$ is:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

Substituting the values for one asteroid:

$$g = \frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (2.00 \times 10^{12})}{(2.00 \times 10^3)^2}$$

$$g \approx 3.34 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the gravitational field from one asteroid.

steps.title: Apply vector subtraction
steps.content:
Gravitational fields always point toward the mass creating them. At the midpoint:
1. Asteroid 1 pulls with field $g_1$ to the left.
2. Asteroid 2 pulls with field $g_2$ to the right.

Since the masses and distances are identical, $|g_1| = |g_2|$. Because they are in opposite directions, we subtract them to find the net field:

$$g_{net} = g_1 - g_2 = 0\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$$

Recognizing that the fields cancel out earns the first mark ($M1$), and the final result of zero earns the second mark ($A1$).

parts.content: Explain whether a test mass placed at the midpoint would accelerate and in which direction.
parts.markscheme:
State that there is no acceleration as the net force is zero <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the midpoint position
steps.content: The question asks about a test mass placed at the **midpoint** between two identical asteroids. Since the total distance between the asteroids is $4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$, the test mass is exactly $2.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$ away from each asteroid.

steps.title: Analyze gravitational force magnitudes
steps.content:
We use Newton's law of gravitation to determine the force exerted by each asteroid on the test mass:

$$F = G\frac{m_{1}m_{2}}{r^{2}}$$

Because the two asteroids are **identical** (same mass $M$) and the distance $r$ to the midpoint is the same for both, the magnitude of the gravitational force from the left asteroid ($F_L$) is exactly equal to the magnitude of the force from the right asteroid ($F_R$).

steps.title: Sum the force vectors
steps.content:
Gravitational force is always attractive. Therefore:
- The left asteroid pulls the test mass to the left.
- The right asteroid pulls the test mass to the right.

Since these two force vectors have equal magnitudes but point in opposite directions, their vector sum (the net force) is zero:

$$F_{net} = F_R - F_L = 0$$

steps.title: Determine the acceleration
steps.content:
Using Newton's Second Law from the data booklet:

$$F = ma$$

Since the net force $F$ acting on the test mass is zero, the acceleration $a$ must also be zero. 

**Conclusion:** The test mass would **not accelerate** because the net force is zero.

steps.title: Determine distance to midpoint
steps.content:
To find the gravitational field at the midpoint, we first need the distance $r$ from either asteroid to that point. Since the asteroids are separated by $4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$, the midpoint is exactly half that distance away.

$$r = \frac{4.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}}{2} = 2.00 \times 10^3\ \text{m}$$

steps.title: Calculate individual field strengths
steps.content:
Using the formula for gravitational field strength from the data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

Since both asteroids have the same mass $M = 2.00 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kg}$ and are at the same distance $r$, the magnitude of the field produced by each asteroid ($g_1$ and $g_2$) is the same.

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation for g using G, M, and r.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation for g using G, M, and r.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the gravitational field strength value.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the gravitational field strength from one asteroid.

steps.title: Apply superposition principle
steps.content:
Gravitational field strength is a vector quantity that points toward the mass creating it. At the midpoint:
1. The field from the first asteroid ($g_1$) points toward the first asteroid.
2. The field from the second asteroid ($g_2$) points toward the second asteroid (the opposite direction).

Because the asteroids are identical and the distances are equal, these two vectors have equal magnitude but opposite directions. Their vector sum, the net gravitational field ($g_{\text{net}}$), is zero:

$$g_{\text{net}} = g_1 - g_2 = 0$$

steps.title: Relate field to acceleration
steps.content:
The gravitational force on a test mass $m$ is given by $F = m g_{\text{net}}$. Since the net field is zero, the net force is also zero ($F = 0$).

According to Newton's Second Law:
$$F = ma$$

If the net force is zero, the acceleration $a$ must also be zero. Therefore, the test mass would **not accelerate** in any direction.

steps.title: Analyze the physical symmetry
steps.content:
To solve this qualitatively, we first look at the properties of the two asteroids. The question states we have **two identical asteroids**, meaning they both have the same mass ($M = 2.00 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kg}$). 

Because they are identical, they generate gravitational fields of exactly the same strength at equal distances from their centers.

steps.title: Evaluate the midpoint position
steps.content:
A test mass is **placed at the midpoint**. In terms of geometry, the midpoint is the unique point that is exactly the same distance away from both asteroid centers. 

Since the masses are the same and the distances to the test mass are the same, the magnitude of the gravitational force exerted by each asteroid on the test mass must be identical.

steps.title: Consider force directions
steps.content:
Gravity is an attractive force. 
- The asteroid on the left pulls the test mass to the left.
- The asteroid on the right pulls the test mass to the right.

Because of the perfect symmetry of the setup, these two force vectors are equal in magnitude but point in exactly opposite directions. In physics, we say there is no "preferred direction" for the mass to move, as neither side exerts a stronger pull than the other.

steps.title: Determine acceleration
steps.content:
Using Newton's Second Law from the data booklet:

$$F = ma$$

Since the two gravitational forces cancel each other out perfectly, the net force ($F_{net}$) acting on the test mass is zero. If the net force is zero, the acceleration ($a$) must also be zero. 

Therefore, the test mass **will not accelerate**.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational potential at points X and Y.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the gravitational potential at point X: $V_X = -\frac{GM}{r_X} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.00 \times 10^{24}}{1.50 \times 10^7} = -1.33 \times 10^7\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate the gravitational potential at point Y: $V_Y = -\frac{GM}{r_Y} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.00 \times 10^{24}}{3.00 \times 10^7} = -6.67 \times 10^6\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the potential formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational potential at a point in space, we use the formula for gravitational potential $V$ due to a point mass $M$. This concept is covered in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**.

The formula is:

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

Where:
- $G$ is the gravitational constant ($6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{kg}^{-2}$)
- $M$ is the mass of the object creating the field
- $r$ is the distance from the center of the mass to the point

steps.title: Calculate potential at point X
steps.content:
Substitute the values for $G$, $M$, and the radius at point X ($r_X$) into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} V_X &= -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.00 \times 10^{24}}{1.50 \times 10^7} \\ V_X &= -1.334 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $V_X = -1.33 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$. Note that gravitational potential is always negative (or zero at infinity).

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen using the 'EE' key for powers of 10.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate gravitational potential at point X.

steps.title: Calculate potential at point Y
steps.content:
Now, repeat the process for point Y using its distance $r_Y$:

$$\begin{aligned} V_Y &= -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.00 \times 10^{24}}{3.00 \times 10^7} \\ V_Y &= -6.67 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

As point Y is further away from the mass than point X, the potential is less negative (closer to zero).

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

steps.content:
The gravitational potentials are:
- At point X: $V_X = -1.33 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$
- At point Y: $V_Y = -6.67 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$

steps.title: Identify the Potential Formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational potential at a point in space, we use the formula for gravitational potential ($V$) from the fields section of your data booklet. This formula describes the potential energy per unit mass at a distance $r$ from a mass $M$.

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

steps.title: Calculate Potential at X
steps.content:
First, we calculate the potential at point X by substituting the given values: $M = 3.00 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$, $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{N m}^2\ \text{kg}^{-2}$, and $r_X = 1.50 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$.

$$\begin{aligned} V_X &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (3.00 \times 10^{24})}{1.50 \times 10^7} \\ V_X &= -1.334 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $V_X = -1.33 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.

gdc.instructions: Type the expression -(6.67*10^-11 * 3.00*10^24) / (1.50*10^7) into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter -(6.67E-11 * 3.00E24) / 1.50E7 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point X.

steps.title: Analyze the Radial Distance
steps.content:
Next, let's look at the distance for point Y. Point Y is at $r_Y = 3.00 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$. 

Comparing this to $r_X$, we see that:
$$\frac{r_Y}{r_X} = \frac{3.00 \times 10^7}{1.50 \times 10^7} = 2$$

So, the distance has exactly doubled ($r_Y = 2r_X$).

steps.title: Apply Proportional Reasoning
steps.content:
Since $V = -\frac{GM}{r}$, we can see that $V$ is inversely proportional to $r$ ($V \propto \frac{1}{r}$).

If the distance $r$ doubles, the magnitude of the potential must be halved. Therefore:

$$V_Y = \frac{V_X}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} V_Y &= \frac{-1.334 \times 10^7}{2} \\ V_Y &= -6.67 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Take the previous answer and divide by 2.

steps.content:
The gravitational potentials at points X and Y are:

$$V_X = -1.33 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$$
$$V_Y = -6.67 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify the approach
steps.content:
To find the work done in moving the mass, we calculate the change in gravitational potential energy between point X and point Y. The work done by an external agent is given by:

$$W = \Delta E_p = E_{p, \text{final}} - E_{p, \text{initial}}$$

We will use the formula for gravitational potential energy from the **Fields** section of the data booklet, adapted for point masses:

$$E_p = -\frac{GMm}{r}$$

steps.title: Calculate energy at X
steps.content:
First, let's calculate the initial gravitational potential energy ($E_{p,X}$) at point X using the given values:
- $M = 3.00 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$
- $m = 0.20\ \text{kg}$
- $r_X = 1.50 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$

$$E_{p,X} = -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(3.00 \times 10^{24})(0.20)}{1.50 \times 10^7}$$

$$E_{p,X} \approx -2.668 \times 10^6\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Calculate energy at Y
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the final gravitational potential energy ($E_{p,Y}$) at point Y where $r_Y = 3.00 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$:

$$E_{p,Y} = -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(3.00 \times 10^{24})(0.20)}{3.00 \times 10^7}$$

$$E_{p,Y} \approx -1.334 \times 10^6\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Solve with GDC
steps.content: You can perform these calculations efficiently on your graphing calculator to avoid power-of-ten errors.

gdc.instructions: Use the scientific notation format and calculate the final energy minus the initial energy.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression for the difference directly using the 'E' key for scientific notation (2nd + ,).

desmos.instructions: Define the constants and evaluate the expression for the change in energy.

calculator.description: Calculate the work done by finding the difference in potential energies: $E_{p,Y} - E_{p,X}$

steps.title: Determine work done
steps.content:
Now, find the difference to determine the work done ($W$):

$$\begin{aligned} W &= E_{p,Y} - E_{p,X} \\ W &= (-1.334 \times 10^6) - (-2.668 \times 10^6) \\ W &= 1.334 \times 10^6\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures as per the markscheme, we get:

$$W = 1.33 \times 10^6\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Identify the strategy
steps.content:
To find the work done, we will use the **Gravitational Potential Difference** approach. Work done ($W$) in moving a mass ($m$) is equal to the product of the mass and the change in gravitational potential ($\Delta V_g$):

$$W = m \Delta V_g = m(V_Y - V_X)$$

First, we need to calculate the gravitational potential at both points X and Y using the formula:

$$V_g = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

We are given:
- $m = 0.20\ \text{kg}$
- $M = 3.00 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$
- $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{N m}^2 \text{kg}^{-2}$

steps.title: Calculate potential at X
steps.content:
Point X is at a distance $r_X = 1.50 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$. Substituting the values into the potential formula:

$$V_X = -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (3.00 \times 10^{24})}{1.50 \times 10^7}$$

$$V_X \approx -1.334 \times 10^7\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point X.

steps.title: Calculate potential at Y
steps.content:
Point Y is at a distance $r_Y = 3.00 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$. Substituting the values:

$$V_Y = -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (3.00 \times 10^{24})}{3.00 \times 10^7}$$

$$V_Y = -6.67 \times 10^6\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point Y.

steps.title: Determine total work done
steps.content:
Now we calculate the work done using $W = m(V_Y - V_X)$. 

$$\begin{aligned} W &= 0.20 \times [(-6.67 \times 10^6) - (-1.334 \times 10^7)] \\ W &= 0.20 \times (6.67 \times 10^6) \\ W &= 1.334 \times 10^6 \, \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to significant figures, we get $1.33 \times 10^6\ \text{J}$.

calculator.description: Calculate the final work done.
