- Calculate the average field strength: $g = 0.445\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Link field and potential
steps.content:
To find the average gravitational field strength $g$, we use the relationship between gravitational potential $V$ and distance $r$. The magnitude of the field strength is equal to the potential gradient:

$$g = \left| \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r} \right|$$

This concept is explored in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**, specifically the section on gravitational potential (HL).

steps.title: Calculate potential at X
steps.content:
First, we calculate the gravitational potential $V_X$ at point X using the formula from the data booklet:

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

Substituting the values for point X ($r = 1.50 \times 10^7\text{ m}$):

$$V_X = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.00 \times 10^{24}}{1.50 \times 10^7} = -1.334 \times 10^7\text{ J kg}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the fraction template and scientific notation.

ti84.instructions: Enter the formula components. Use (-) for the negative sign and [EE] for scientific notation.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point X.

steps.title: Calculate potential at Y
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the potential $V_Y$ at point Y ($r = 3.00 \times 10^7\text{ m}$):

$$V_Y = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.00 \times 10^{24}}{3.00 \times 10^7} = -6.67 \times 10^6\text{ J kg}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Substitute the new radius into the previous expression.

ti84.instructions: Enter the formula components.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point Y.

steps.title: Determine average field strength
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the change in potential ($\Delta V$) and change in distance ($\Delta r$) to find the gradient:

$$\Delta V = V_Y - V_X = (-6.67 \times 10^6) - (-1.334 \times 10^7) = 6.67 \times 10^6\text{ J kg}^{-1}$$
$$\Delta r = 3.00 \times 10^7 - 1.50 \times 10^7 = 1.50 \times 10^7\text{ m}$$

Now, substitute these into the gradient expression:

$$g = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r} = \frac{6.67 \times 10^6}{1.50 \times 10^7} \approx 0.445\text{ N kg}^{-1}$$

This gives us the average field strength over that distance.

gdc.instructions: Perform the division as shown.

ti84.instructions: Divide the change in potential by the change in distance.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the final ratio.

calculator.description: Calculate the final field strength value.

steps.title: Define the Work-Energy approach
steps.content:
To find the average gravitational field strength $g$, we use the **Work-Energy Relationship**. In a gravitational field, the average field strength is equivalent to the work done per unit mass ($m$) per unit distance ($\Delta r$). 

Mathematically, since work done is the change in gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p$):

$$g = \frac{\Delta E_p}{m \cdot \Delta r}$$

This is equivalent to the gravitational potential gradient: $g = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$.

steps.title: Calculate potential energy change
steps.content:
First, we calculate the gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) at points X and Y using the formula from **Topic D.1.3**:

$$E_p = -\frac{GMm}{r}$$

For Point X ($r = 1.50 \times 10^7\text{ m}$):
$$E_{p,X} = -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(3.00 \times 10^{24})(0.20)}{1.50 \times 10^7} = -2.668 \times 10^6\text{ J}$$

For Point Y ($r = 3.00 \times 10^7\text{ m}$):
$$E_{p,Y} = -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(3.00 \times 10^{24})(0.20)}{3.00 \times 10^7} = -1.334 \times 10^6\text{ J}$$

Now, find the change (Work Done):
$$\Delta E_p = E_{p,Y} - E_{p,X} = (-1.334 \times 10^6) - (-2.668 \times 10^6) = 1.334 \times 10^6\text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate potential energy at both radii and subtract X from Y.

ti84.instructions: Enter -(6.67*10^-11 * 3.00*10^24 * 0.20) / (1.50*10^7) for X and change denominator to 3.00*10^7 for Y.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the difference between the two potential energy values.

calculator.description: Calculate Ep at point X and Y, then find the difference.

steps.title: Determine average field strength
steps.content:
Next, determine the distance moved ($\Delta r$) and apply the field strength relationship:

$$\Delta r = 3.00 \times 10^7 - 1.50 \times 10^7 = 1.50 \times 10^7\text{ m}$$

Using the work-energy principle:
$$g = \frac{\text{Work}}{m \cdot \Delta r} = \frac{1.334 \times 10^6}{0.20 \cdot (1.50 \times 10^7)}$$

$$g = 0.4446...\text{ N kg}^{-1}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:
$$g = 0.445\text{ N kg}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the division to find field strength.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the final quotient.

calculator.description: Calculate the average field strength by dividing the energy change by mass and distance.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The average field strength is **$0.445\text{ N kg}^{-1}$**.

**Marking Breakdown:**
- **M1**: Correct substitution of values into the potential gradient or work-energy expression.
- **A1**: Correct final value with appropriate units.

steps.title: Define the potential gradient
steps.content:
The gravitational field strength $g$ is defined as the negative gradient of the gravitational potential $V$ with respect to distance $r$. To find the average field strength between two points $X$ and $Y$, we use the change in potential ($\Delta V$) over the change in distance ($\Delta r$):

$$g = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$$

steps.title: Express potential difference algebraically
steps.content:
The gravitational potential at a distance $r$ is given by $V = -\frac{GM}{r}$. Let's express the difference in potential between point $Y$ ($r_2$) and point $X$ ($r_1$):

$$\Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = \left(-\frac{GM}{r_2}\right) - \left(-\frac{GM}{r_1}\right)$$

Factoring out $GM$, we get:

$$\Delta V = GM \left( \frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2} \right)$$

Using a common denominator for the terms in the bracket:

$$\Delta V = GM \left( \frac{r_2 - r_1}{r_1 r_2} \right)$$

steps.title: Derive the average field formula
steps.content:
Now we substitute our algebraic expression for $\Delta V$ back into the gradient formula $g = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$, where $\Delta r = r_2 - r_1$:

$$g = \frac{GM \left( \frac{r_2 - r_1}{r_1 r_2} \right)}{r_2 - r_1}$$

The term $(r_2 - r_1)$ cancels out from the numerator and denominator, leaving us with a single simplified expression for the average field strength:

$$g = \frac{GM}{r_1 r_2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the numerical value
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the known values into our derived formula:
- $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^2 \text{kg}^{-2}$
- $M = 3.00 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$
- $r_1 = 1.50 \times 10^7 \text{ m}$
- $r_2 = 3.00 \times 10^7 \text{ m}$

$$g = \frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(3.00 \times 10^{24})}{(1.50 \times 10^7)(3.00 \times 10^7)}$$

$$g \approx 0.445 \text{ N kg}^{-1}$$

This method earns **[1M]** for the correct substitution into the gradient relationship and **[1A]** for the final result.

parts.content: Sketch the gravitational potential $V$ versus radial distance $r$ from the mass. Indicate on the graph the direction of increasing potential and the relationship to field strength.

- Draw a graph with $V$ on the vertical axis and $r$ on the horizontal axis showing a negative curve approaching zero asymptotically from below <marks>A1</marks>
- Annotate the graph to show the direction of increasing potential (towards $V=0$) and that the gradient (slope) of the curve represents the field strength $g$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the mathematical function
steps.content:
To sketch the graph correctly, we start with the algebraic definition of gravitational potential $V$ for a point mass $M$. From **Topic D.1.3 (Gravitational Potential)**, we know:

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

Since $G$ and $M$ are positive constants and $r$ is always positive, the potential $V$ will always have a **negative value**. This tells us the graph will be located in the fourth quadrant (below the horizontal axis).

steps.title: Analyze the curve's behavior
steps.content:
Using functional analysis, we can determine the shape of the curve $V = -\frac{GM}{r}$:

1.  **As $r$ increases**: The fraction $\frac{GM}{r}$ gets smaller. Because of the negative sign, $V$ becomes "less negative" (e.g., moving from $-10$ to $-5$). This means $V$ is **increasing** as we move away from the mass.
2.  **Asymptotic behavior**: As $r \to \infty$, the value of $V$ approaches $0$. Therefore, the $r$-axis is a horizontal asymptote.

gdc.instructions: Graph the function f(x) = -1/x for x > 0.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = -1/X (using a simple model where GM=1).
3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=10, Ymin=-5, Ymax=1.
4. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation into the input bar to see the asymptotic behavior.

calculator.description: Visualize the negative reciprocal shape of the potential function.

steps.title: Relate gradient to field strength
steps.content:
The question asks for the relationship to gravitational field strength $g$. In physics, the field strength is defined as the negative gradient of the potential:

$$g = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$$

On your sketch, the **magnitude of the gradient (slope)** at any point represents the gravitational field strength $g$ at that radial distance.

steps.title: Sketch and annotate
steps.content:
Now, combine these features into a final sketch:

1.  **Axes**: Draw $V$ on the vertical axis and $r$ on the horizontal axis.
2.  **Curve**: Draw a smooth curve that starts deep in the negative region and curves upward, getting closer and closer to the $r$-axis without ever touching it.
3.  **Increasing potential**: Draw an arrow pointing upwards (towards $V=0$) and label it "increasing potential".
4.  **Gradient**: Draw a tangent line at a point and label that its gradient represents $g$.

highlights.text: Draw a graph with $V$ on the vertical axis and $r$ on the horizontal axis showing a negative curve approaching zero asymptotically from below
Annotate the graph to show the direction of increasing potential (towards $V=0$) and that the gradient (slope) of the curve represents the field strength $g$

steps.title: Identify the Potential Formula
steps.content:
To sketch the graph accurately, we first identify the formula for gravitational potential $V$ at a distance $r$ from a point mass $M$. This concept is covered in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**. 

From the definitions of gravitational potential, we use:

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

Notice that the potential is always negative and approaches zero as $r$ becomes very large.

steps.title: Calculate Potential at Point X
steps.content:
Let's calculate the specific value of the potential at point X using the provided values for $M$ and $r_X$.

$$\begin{aligned} V_X &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (3.00 \times 10^{24})}{1.50 \times 10^7} \\ V_X &\approx -1.33 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the division in the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to get the result.

calculator.description: Calculate gravitational potential at point X.

steps.title: Calculate Potential at Point Y
steps.content:
Now we calculate the potential at point Y, which is further away. Since the distance $r$ has doubled, we expect the magnitude of the potential to be halved.

$$\begin{aligned} V_Y &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (3.00 \times 10^{24})}{3.00 \times 10^7} \\ V_Y &\approx -6.67 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the division in the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to get the result.

calculator.description: Calculate gravitational potential at point Y.

steps.title: Analyze Trends for Sketching
steps.content:
Comparing our results:
- At $r = 1.50 \times 10^7\text{ m}$, $V \approx -1.33 \times 10^7\text{ J kg}^{-1}$
- At $r = 3.00 \times 10^7\text{ m}$, $V \approx -0.67 \times 10^7\text{ J kg}^{-1}$

As $r$ increases, $V$ becomes **less negative** (moves closer to zero). This means the potential is **increasing** as we move away from the mass. The curve will be in the negative quadrant, starting deep and curving upwards toward the horizontal axis ($V=0$).

steps.title: Relationship to Field Strength
steps.content:
Finally, we must relate the curve to the gravitational field strength $g$. In IB Physics, the magnitude of the gravitational field strength is equal to the gradient (slope) of the potential graph:

$$g = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$$

Notice that as $r$ increases, the curve becomes flatter (less steep). This correctly indicates that the field strength $g$ decreases as distance from the mass increases.

steps.title: Draw and Annotate
steps.content:
When sketching your final graph for the exam:
1. Draw a curve in the lower-right quadrant (negative $V$, positive $r$) that approaches the $r$-axis but never quite touches it (asymptotic behavior).
2. Add an arrow pointing upwards (towards $V=0$) labeled **"Increasing potential"**.
3. Add a label to the curve stating **"gradient = field strength g"** (or $g = -\frac{dV}{dr}$).

This will earn both marks according to the markscheme.

parts.content: Explain what gravitational field lines represent and describe their properties around a point mass and a uniform spherical mass like Earth.
parts.markscheme:
- **Definition**: Gravitational field lines represent the direction and relative strength of the gravitational field around a mass, pointing towards the mass (indicating an attractive force) <marks>A1</marks>

- **Around a point mass**: Field lines radiate inward towards the center of the mass and are radially symmetrical <marks>A1</marks>

- **Around a uniform spherical mass**: Similar to a point mass, the field lines radiate inward symmetrically from the center <marks>A1</marks>

- **Field strength and density**: The field lines are denser (closer together) near the mass, indicating a stronger gravitational field, and spread out as they move away, indicating a decrease in field strength <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<examTip>When drawing field lines, ensure they never cross and always point inward towards the mass</examTip>

steps.title: Define gravitational field lines
steps.content:
In IB Physics, we use field lines as a visual tool to describe the **gravitational field** around a mass. These lines represent two things:
1. **Direction**: The arrows on the lines indicate the direction of the force that a small "test mass" would experience. Since gravity is always an **attractive force**, these lines always point **inwards toward the mass**.
2. **Relative Strength**: The spacing between the lines tells us how strong the field is at that location.

highlights.text: represent the direction and relative strength of the gravitational field around a mass, pointing towards the mass (indicating an attractive force)

steps.title: Properties of a point mass
steps.content:
For a **point mass**, the gravitational field is **radially symmetrical**. This means the field lines look like the spokes of a wheel, all directed straight toward the single point where the mass is located. 

Because the mass is concentrated at a single point, the field lines originate from infinity and converge at that center point.

highlights.text: Field lines radiate inward towards the center of the mass and are radially symmetrical

steps.title: Properties of spherical masses
steps.content:
For a **uniform spherical mass** (like the Earth), the field outside the sphere is identical to that of a point mass. 

The lines are perpendicular to the surface of the sphere and **radiate inward symmetrically** toward the center. This shows that, from the perspective of an object in space, the Earth's gravity behaves as if all its mass were concentrated at its geometric center.

highlights.text: the field lines radiate inward symmetrically from the center

steps.title: Field strength and density
steps.content:
The **density of the field lines** (how close they are to each other) represents the gravitational field strength ($g$). 

Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

We can see that as the distance ($r$) increases, the field strength ($g$) decreases. Qualitatively, this is shown by the field lines **spreading out** as they move further away from the mass. Closer to the surface or the point mass, the lines are **denser**, indicating a stronger field.

highlights.text: The field lines are denser (closer together) near the mass, indicating a stronger gravitational field, and spread out as they move away, indicating a decrease in field strength

steps.title: Define gravitational field lines
steps.content:
Gravitational field lines are a visual tool used to represent the gravitational field. They tell us two main things:
1. **Direction**: The arrows on the lines show the direction of the force that a small "test mass" would experience. Since gravity is always attractive, these lines always point **inwards** toward the mass.
2. **Relative Strength**: The spacing between the lines indicates how strong the field is. Where lines are closer together, the field is stronger.

highlights.text: Gravitational field lines represent the direction and relative strength of the gravitational field around a mass, pointing towards the mass (indicating an attractive force)

steps.title: Justify radial symmetry
steps.content:
To understand the shape of the field around a point mass, we look at the formula for gravitational field strength from your data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

This formula shows that the magnitude of $g$ depends only on the distance $r$ from the center of the mass $M$. Because the force is directed towards the center from every direction in 3D space, the field lines must be **radial** (like spokes on a wheel) and point inward.

steps.title: Compare point and spherical masses
steps.content:
For a uniform spherical mass like the Earth, the field lines outside the surface look exactly like those of a point mass. 

This is because, for any point outside the sphere, the mass behaves as if all its mass $M$ were concentrated at its geometric center. Therefore, the lines radiate inward symmetrically toward the center for both cases.

highlights.text: Field lines radiate inward towards the center of the mass and are radially symmetrical

steps.title: Explain line density
steps.content:
We can use the inverse-square law relationship, $g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$, to justify the density of the lines:

- **Near the mass**: $r$ is small, so $g$ is large. To represent this high strength, we draw the field lines **closer together** (higher density).
- **Far from the mass**: As $r$ increases, $g$ decreases rapidly. As radial lines move outward, they naturally spread apart, representing the **decrease in field strength**.

This perfectly matches the visual representation where line density indicates field magnitude.

highlights.text: The field lines are denser (closer together) near the mass, indicating a stronger gravitational field, and spread out as they move away, indicating a decrease in field strength

parts.content:
Calculate the gravitational field strength at a height of $3.2 \times 10^6$ m above Earth's surface, given that:

- The gravitational field strength $g$ at Earth's surface is approximately $9.8$ N/kg
- The radius of Earth $R$ is $6.4 \times 10^6$ m
parts.markscheme:
- Recall formula for gravitational field strength at distance $r$ from Earth's center: $g=\frac{G M}{r^2}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate total distance $r$ from Earth's center:
$$r=R+h=6.4 \times 10^6 \text{ m}+3.2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}=9.6 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Use inverse-square relationship for field strength:
$$\frac{g_{\text{new}}}{g_{\text{surface}}}=\left(\frac{R}{r}\right)^2$$

Substituting values:
$$g_{\text{new}}=9.8 \text{ N/kg} \times\left(\frac{6.4 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}{9.6 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}\right)^2$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate final answer: $g_{\text{new}} \approx 4.36 \text{ N/kg}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Calculate distance from center
steps.content:
In gravitational problems, the distance $r$ must always be measured from the center of the mass. The question gives us the height above the surface, so we add the Earth's radius to find the total distance.

$$\begin{aligned} r &= R + h \\ r &= 6.4 \times 10^6 \text{ m} + 3.2 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \\ r &= 9.6 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This is a crucial first step that earns you **1 mark**.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
The gravitational field strength formula from the data booklet shows how $g$ depends on distance. Since the mass of Earth ($M$) and the gravitational constant ($G$) are fixed, we can see that $g$ is inversely proportional to the square of the distance ($r^2$).

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2} \implies g \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$$

This means if we know the field strength at the surface ($g_s$ at distance $R$), we can set up a ratio for the new field strength ($g_{new}$ at distance $r$).

steps.title: Set up the ratio
steps.content:
Using the inverse square law relationship, we can relate the two points:

$$\frac{g_{\text{new}}}{g_{\text{surface}}} = \frac{R^2}{r^2} = \left(\frac{R}{r}\right)^2$$

Substituting our known values:

$$\frac{g_{\text{new}}}{9.8} = \left(\frac{6.4 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^6}\right)^2$$

Setting up this substitution correctly earns you the next **mark**.

steps.title: Simplify and solve
steps.content:
Now we calculate the final value. Notice that the $10^6$ terms cancel out, and $6.4 / 9.6$ simplifies to $2/3$.

$$\begin{aligned} g_{\text{new}} &= 9.8 \times \left(\frac{6.4}{9.6}\right)^2 \\ g_{\text{new}} &= 9.8 \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 \\ g_{\text{new}} &= 9.8 \times \frac{4}{9} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to an appropriate number of significant figures gives the final result of $4.36 \text{ N/kg}$.

gdc.instructions: Type 9.8 * (6.4 / 9.6)^2 into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 9.8 * (6.4 / 9.6)^2 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 9.8 * (6.4 / 9.6)^2 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final gravitational field strength value.

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational field strength at a certain distance from a planet, we use the formula for gravitational field strength from the **Fields** section of your data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

In this method, we will first determine the mass of the Earth ($M$) using the values we know for the surface, and then use that mass to find the field strength at the new height.

steps.title: Calculate Earth's mass
steps.content:
At Earth's surface, the distance from the center is equal to the radius ($r = R$). We can rearrange the field strength formula to solve for the Earth's mass ($M$):

$$\begin{aligned} g &= \frac{GM}{R^2} \\ M &= \frac{gR^2}{G} \end{aligned}$$

Using the surface field strength of $9.8 \text{ N/kg}$, the Earth's radius of $6.4 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$, and the gravitational constant $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{kg}^{-2}$ from the data booklet:

$$M = \frac{9.8 \times (6.4 \times 10^6)^2}{6.67 \times 10^{-11}}$$

Substituting these values gives $M \approx 6.02 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the mass of Earth on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find M.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of the Earth using surface values.

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
The gravitational field strength depends on the distance from the **center** of the mass. We must add the height above the surface ($h$) to the Earth's radius ($R$):

$$\begin{aligned} r &= R + h \\ r &= 6.4 \times 10^6 \text{ m} + 3.2 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \\ r &= 9.6 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This step is crucial for earning the **A1** mark in the markscheme.

steps.title: Final field strength calculation
steps.content:
Now, substitute the mass of the Earth ($M$) and the total distance ($r$) back into the field strength formula to find the new value ($g_{\text{new}}$):

$$g_{\text{new}} = \frac{G \times M}{r^2}$$

$$g_{\text{new}} = \frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6.018 \times 10^{24}}{(9.6 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$g_{\text{new}} \approx 4.36 \text{ N/kg}$$

In an exam, you would earn marks for recalling the formula (**M1**), calculating the correct total distance (**A1**), showing the substitution (**M1**), and providing the final answer with units (**A1**).

gdc.instructions: Substitute the calculated mass and distance into the gravitational field formula.

ti84.instructions: Use the result for M from the previous step and divide by the square of the total distance.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the final field strength.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational field strength at the new height.

parts.content: Draw a diagram showing the gravitational field lines between Earth and a smaller mass, such as the Moon, at a point where the gravitational forces due to Earth and the Moon cancel each other out. Briefly describe the nature of this point and explain what would happen to a small object placed there.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct diagram showing Earth and Moon with gravitational field lines converging towards each body and meeting at L1 point <marks>A1</marks>

![Gravitational Field Lines Earth-Moon](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/eabdce3a-91b7-4e20-8ca5-30dcf4cbbae2.jpeg)

- Identification that L1 is where gravitational forces from Earth and Moon are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, resulting in zero net force <marks>A1</marks>

- Explanation that an object at L1 would:
  - Theoretically remain stationary relative to Earth-Moon system
  - Be in unstable equilibrium
  - Drift away if slightly displaced due to perturbations <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Diagram must clearly show field lines getting closer together near each body to indicate increasing field strength</note>

<note>Accept alternative valid explanations for instability that demonstrate understanding of the equilibrium nature</note>

steps.title: Draw the field lines
steps.content:
To start, we need to visualize the gravitational environment between the Earth and the Moon. Gravitational field lines always point toward the mass creating the field. Since both bodies attract a test mass, the field lines will converge toward the centers of both Earth and the Moon.

Between the two bodies, there is a specific region where the fields "oppose" each other. Your diagram should show:
1. Field lines pointing into Earth and into the Moon.
2. Lines getting closer together (denser) near the surface of each body to show the field is stronger there.
3. A specific point between them (closer to the Moon) where no field lines pass through—this represents the **L1 Lagrange point**.

highlights.text: gravitational field lines converging towards each body

steps.title: Identify the Null Point
steps.content:
The point where the forces cancel out is known as a **null point** (specifically the L1 point). At this location, a small mass $m$ experiences two gravitational force vectors:
- $\vec{F}_E$ pulling it toward the Earth.
- $\vec{F}_M$ pulling it toward the Moon.

Using the formula for gravitational force from the data booklet:

$$F = G\frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}$$

At the null point, these two vectors are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction. Therefore, the net force $\vec{F}_{net}$ is zero:

$$\vec{F}_{net} = \vec{F}_E + \vec{F}_M = 0$$

highlights.text: gravitational forces due to Earth and the Moon cancel each other out

steps.title: Analyze Stability
steps.content:
While the net force is zero at the L1 point, this equilibrium is **unstable**. We can explain this by looking at how the force vectors change if the object is slightly displaced:

1. **Displacement toward Earth:** The distance to Earth ($r_E$) decreases, so the force $F_E$ increases ($F \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$). Simultaneously, the distance to the Moon increases, so $F_M$ decreases. The net force is no longer zero and now points toward Earth, pulling the object further away from the equilibrium point.
2. **Displacement toward Moon:** The same logic applies; $F_M$ would become stronger than $F_E$, pulling the object toward the Moon.

Because any small disturbance causes the object to drift away rather than return to the center, we describe the nature of this point as an **unstable equilibrium**.

highlights.text: explain what would happen to a small object placed there

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To ensure you get all 3 marks, your answer should cover:
- **Mark 1:** A clear diagram showing lines converging to both masses with a gap at the L1 point.
- **Mark 2:** A statement that the gravitational forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction at this point.
- **Mark 3:** An explanation that the equilibrium is unstable and the object will drift away if displaced.

This topic is part of **Topic D.1 (Gravitational Fields)**. Reviewing the relationship between field strength and distance is key to understanding why this point is unstable!

steps.title: Visualize the Field Lines
steps.content:
To draw the diagram, remember that gravitational field lines always point **towards** the mass creating the field. Since the Earth is significantly more massive than the Moon, the point where the fields cancel (the $L1$ point) will be located much closer to the Moon.

In your diagram:
1. Draw field lines pointing radially inward toward both the Earth and the Moon.
2. Ensure the lines are denser near the surfaces to show a stronger field.
3. Between the two bodies, show the lines "avoiding" a specific point ($L1$) where the net field strength is zero.

highlights.text: Correct diagram showing Earth and Moon with gravitational field lines converging towards each body and meeting at L1 point

steps.title: Identify the L1 Point
steps.content:
The point described is the **first Lagrange point ($L1$)**. In terms of field theory, this is the location where the gravitational field strength from the Earth ($g_E$) and the Moon ($g_M$) are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction.

Using the formula for gravitational field strength from your data booklet:

$$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$

At the $L1$ point, the net field strength is:

$$g_{net} = g_E - g_M = 0$$

Because the net field is zero, the net force ($F = mg$) on any object placed there is also zero.

highlights.text: Identification that L1 is where gravitational forces from Earth and Moon are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, resulting in zero net force

steps.title: Analyze Stability
steps.content:
While the net force is zero at $L1$, the equilibrium is **unstable**. 

In potential theory, we can think of this point as a "saddle point" in the gravitational potential landscape. Along the line connecting the Earth and Moon, the $L1$ point is a local maximum of gravitational potential. 

If a small object is placed at $L1$ and receives a tiny nudge (perturbation) toward either the Earth or the Moon, the gravitational field of that closer body will become stronger than the other. This creates a resultant force that pulls the object further away from $L1$, causing it to "fall" toward one of the masses.

highlights.text: Drift away if slightly displaced due to perturbations

parts.content: Derive the expression for Kepler's third law of planetary motion.
parts.markscheme:
- Equate gravitational force to centripetal force: 
  $$\frac{GMm}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{GMm}{r^2} = m\omega^2r$$
  <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute $v = \frac{2\pi r}{T}$ or $\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$ into the equation:
  $$\frac{GM}{r} = \left(\frac{2\pi r}{T}\right)^2$$
  <marks>M1</marks>

- Rearrange to obtain the final expression:
  $$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM}r^3 \implies T^2 \propto r^3$$
  <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Calculate the mass of the star based on the given orbital data.
parts.markscheme:
- Convert the period into seconds:
  $$T = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.15 \times 10^7 \, \text{s}$$
  <marks>A1</marks>

- Rearrange Kepler's third law to solve for mass $M$:
  $$M = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{GT^2}$$
  <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the mass of the star:
  $$M = \frac{4\pi^2 (1.5 \times 10^{11})^3}{6.67 \times 10^{-11} (3.15 \times 10^7)^2} = 2.01 \times 10^{30} \, \text{kg}$$
  <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept answers in the range $2.00 \times 10^{30} \, \text{kg}$ to $2.02 \times 10^{30} \, \text{kg}$.</note>

steps.title: Convert period to seconds
steps.content:
To perform calculations in SI units, we must first convert the orbital period from days into seconds.

$$\begin{aligned} T &= 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours/day} \times 3600 \text{ seconds/hour} \\ T &= 31,536,000 \text{ s} \\ T &\approx 3.15 \times 10^7 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type 365 * 24 * 3600 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 365 * 24 * 3600 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 365 * 24 * 3600 into a new line.

calculator.description: Convert 365 days to seconds.

steps.title: Calculate orbital velocity
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the linear orbital speed $v$ using the formula for the circumference of the orbit divided by the period $T$:

$$v = \frac{2\pi r}{T}$$

Substituting the given radius and our calculated time:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{2 \times \pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m})}{31,536,000 \text{ s}} \\ v &\approx 2.99 \times 10^4 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type (2 * π * 1.5e11) / 31536000 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Press (2 * π * 1.5 * 10^11) / 31536000 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the orbital velocity v.

steps.title: Relate gravity and centripetal motion
steps.content:
In circular motion, the gravitational force provides the necessary centripetal force. We equate the formulas for gravitational force and centripetal force:

$$G\frac{Mm}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

Where:
- $M$ is the mass of the star
- $m$ is the mass of the planet
- $v$ is the orbital velocity

Canceling $m$ and one $r$, we rearrange to solve for $M$:

$$M = \frac{v^2 r}{G}$$

steps.title: Calculate the star's mass
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute the orbital velocity $v$, the orbital radius $r$, and the gravitational constant $G$ into our formula:

$$M = \frac{(2.99 \times 10^4)^2 \times (1.5 \times 10^{11})}{6.67 \times 10^{-11}}$$

Using more precise values from the calculator:

$$M \approx 2.01 \times 10^{30} \text{ kg}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of the star M.

steps.title: Convert period to seconds
steps.content:
To use standard SI units in our physics formulas, we must first convert the orbital period from days into seconds. There are 24 hours in a day and 3,600 seconds in an hour ($60 \times 60$).

$$\begin{aligned} T &= 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h/day} \times 3,600 \text{ s/h} \\ T &= 31,536,000 \text{ s} \\ T &\approx 3.15 \times 10^7 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This step earns you the first mark.

steps.title: Identify Kepler's Third Law
steps.content:
For a circular orbit, the gravitational force provides the centripetal force ($F_g = F_c$). By substituting $v = \frac{2\pi r}{T}$, we derive the relationship for the mass of the central body ($M$):

$$M = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{GT^2}$$

From the Data Booklet, the gravitational constant is:
$$G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{kg}^{-2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the star's mass
steps.content:
Now, substitute the radius $r = 1.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$, the period $T \approx 3.15 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$, and the constant $G$ into the equation:

$$M = \frac{4\pi^2 (1.5 \times 10^{11})^3}{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(3.15 \times 10^7)^2}$$

Performing the calculation gives:
$$M = 2.01 \times 10^{30} \text{ kg}$$

This is approximately the mass of our Sun!

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, enter the numerator and denominator carefully.

ti84.instructions: Enter the formula components. Use the '(' and ')' keys to ensure order of operations. Use the '^' key for powers.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar. Use 'pi' for π and '^' for exponents.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of the star using the orbital data.

steps.title: Convert period to seconds
steps.content:
To perform calculations in standard SI units, we first need to convert the orbital period from days to seconds. 

$$\begin{aligned} T &= 365 \text{ days} \\ T &= 365 \times 24 \text{ hours/day} \times 3600 \text{ seconds/hour} \\ T &\approx 3.15 \times 10^7 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This conversion is a necessary first step in almost all IB mechanics and field problems involving time.

gdc.instructions: Type 365 * 24 * 3600 and press EXE/ENTER.

ti84.instructions: Enter 365 * 24 * 3600 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total seconds in 365 days.

steps.title: Calculate angular velocity
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the angular velocity $\omega$ of the planet. Angular velocity represents the rate of change of the angle per unit time.

Using the relationship:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \frac{2\pi}{31,536,000} \\ \omega &\approx 1.99 \times 10^{-7} \text{ rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type (2 * π) / 31536000 and press EXE/ENTER.

ti84.instructions: Enter (2 * π) / 31536000 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate angular velocity using the period found in Step 0.

steps.title: Relate gravitation and centripetal motion
steps.content:
For a planet in a stable circular orbit, the gravitational force from the star provides the necessary centripetal force. This means the gravitational field strength $g$ at that distance must equal the centripetal acceleration $a$.

From the **Key Physics Equations** in your data booklet:
- Gravitational field strength: $$g = G\frac{M}{r^2}$$
- Centripetal acceleration: $$a = \omega^2 r$$

Setting these equal to each other:
$$\omega^2 r = \frac{GM}{r^2}$$

steps.title: Solve for the star's mass
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to solve for the mass of the star $M$:

$$M = \frac{\omega^2 r^3}{G}$$

Substitute the known values:
- $\omega \approx 1.992 \times 10^{-7} \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
- $r = 1.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$
- $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^2 \text{kg}^{-2}$

$$\begin{aligned} M &= \frac{(1.992 \times 10^{-7})^2 \times (1.5 \times 10^{11})^3}{6.67 \times 10^{-11}} \\ M &\approx 2.01 \times 10^{30} \text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type (1.99238E-7)^2 * (1.5E11)^3 / 6.67E-11 and press EXE/ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the final mass using the derived formula.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
The mass of the star is approximately $2.01 \times 10^{30} \text{ kg}$.

This calculation earned 3 marks:
1.  **A1** for correctly converting the period to seconds ($3.15 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$).
2.  **M1** for identifying the correct physics relationship (equating gravitational and centripetal components).
3.  **A1** for the final numerical answer within the acceptable range ($2.00 \times 10^{30}$ to $2.02 \times 10^{30} \text{ kg}$).

parts.content: Explain how Kepler's third law enhances our understanding of planetary motion and the dynamics of solar systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that it allows determination of relative distances of planets from their host star using orbital periods <marks>A1</marks>

- Explain that it enables the estimation of the mass of the central body (star) when combined with Newton's law of gravitation <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Relate period to distance
steps.content:
Kepler's Third Law, also known as the Law of Harmonies, establishes a proportional relationship between the orbital period ($T$) and the orbital radius ($r$). Specifically, it states that for any planet orbiting a central star:

$$T^2 \propto r^3$$

This is incredibly useful because it allows astronomers to determine the **relative distances** of planets from their host star simply by observing how long they take to complete one orbit (their period).

highlights.text: Explain that it allows determination of relative distances of planets from their host star using orbital periods

steps.title: Synthesize with Newton's laws
steps.content:
To understand the dynamics of the system, we synthesize Kepler's observation with **Newton's Law of Gravitation** and the formula for **centripetal force** found in **Topic D.1 Gravitational fields**.

For a circular orbit, the gravitational force provides the centripetal acceleration:

$$\begin{aligned} F_g &= F_c \\ G\frac{Mm}{r^2} &= m\frac{v^2}{r} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the orbital velocity $$v = \frac{2\pi r}{T}$$ into the equation allows us to derive a constant that links the two laws together.

steps.title: Calculate the central mass
steps.content:
By rearranging the combined equation, we get the mathematical form of Kepler's Third Law:

$$T^2 = \left( \frac{4\pi^2}{GM} \right) r^3$$

In this equation, $M$ is the **mass of the central star**. This synthesis is vital because it enables us to estimate the mass of the star (the central body) by measuring the orbital characteristics ($T$ and $r$) of its planets.

highlights.text: Explain that it enables the estimation of the mass of the central body (star) when combined with Newton's law of gravitation

steps.title: Summarize key impacts
steps.content:
To secure full marks, ensure your explanation covers these two points:
1.  **Relative Distances:** It lets us map out the solar system's scale using orbital periods.
2.  **Mass Determination:** When combined with Newton's laws, it provides a way to calculate the mass of the central body ($M$), which is essential for understanding the overall dynamics and stability of the solar system.

options.markscheme:
- Recall the expression for gravitational field strength at the surface: $g = \frac{GM}{R^2} \Rightarrow GM = gR^2$
- Recall the expression for escape speed from the surface: $v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2GM}{R}}$
- Substitute $GM$ into the escape speed equation: $v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2gR^2}{R}} = \sqrt{2gR}$
- Recognize that for a constant $g$, the escape speed is proportional to the square root of the radius: $v_e \propto \sqrt{R}$
- Use the given ratio $R_Y = 4R_X$ to find the ratio of escape speeds: $\frac{v_Y}{v_X} = \sqrt{\frac{R_Y}{R_X}} = \sqrt{4} = 2$
- Conclude that the escape speed from Planet Y is $2v$.

parts.content: Calculate the orbital speed of the spacecraft.
parts.markscheme:
$r = 6.37 \times 10^6 + 5.00 \times 10^5 = 6.87 \times 10^6\ \text{m}$
$v = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}} = \sqrt{\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 5.97 \times 10^{24}}{6.87 \times 10^6}} = 7612\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
Correct radius 1 mark , correct final speed with unit 1 mark

parts.content: Determine the total mechanical energy of the spacecraft in orbit.
parts.markscheme:
$E_T = -\frac{GMm}{2r} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 5.97 \times 10^{24} \cdot 1200}{2 \cdot 6.87 \times 10^6} = -3.48 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$
Correct use of formula 1 mark , correct final energy with unit 1 mark

parts.content: Calculate the difference in gravitational potential energy between the spacecraft at orbit and on the surface of the Earth.
parts.markscheme:
$
U_{\text{orbit}} = -\frac{GMm}{r} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 5.97 \times 10^{24} \cdot 1200}{6.87 \times 10^6} = -6.96 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$
$U_{\text{surface}} = -\frac{GMm}{R} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 5.97 \times 10^{24} \cdot 1200}{6.37 \times 10^6} = -7.52 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$
$\Delta U = 5.6 \times 10^9\ \text{J}$
Correct potential energies 1 mark , correct energy difference with unit 1 mark

parts.content: If the spacecraft is to escape Earth’s gravity from orbit, calculate the additional speed required (i.e., the difference between escape speed and orbital speed).
parts.markscheme:
$
 v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2GM}{r}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot v_{\text{orbit}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 7612 = 10,764\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
$\Delta v = 10,764 - 7612 = 3152\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
Correct expression or logic 1 mark , correct final $\Delta v$ 1 mark

parts.content: Explain why escape speed is independent of the mass of the escaping object.
parts.markscheme: Escape speed depends only on the gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy balance; since both are proportional to mass, the mass cancels out. 1 mark

labeledSubSubTopics.title: D.1.1 Laws and properties of gravitational fields
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires determining the net gravitational field strength at a midpoint, involving the vector nature and laws governing gravitational fields.

options.content: The gravitational field strength is zero and the gravitational potential is negative.
options.markscheme: Gravitational field strength ($g$) is a vector quantity. At the midpoint of two identical masses, the field vectors point in opposite directions and have equal magnitude ($g = \frac{GM}{(L/2)^2}$), so they cancel out to zero. Gravitational potential ($V$) is a scalar quantity. The total potential is the sum of the potentials from each star: $V_{total} = V_1 + V_2$. Since $V = -\frac{GM}{r}$, the sum of two negative scalars results in a non-zero, negative total potential.

options.content: The gravitational field strength is zero and the gravitational potential is zero.
options.markscheme: This incorrectly assumes that gravitational potential is a vector that cancels at the midpoint, or that potential must be zero where the field strength is zero.

options.content: The gravitational field strength is non-zero and the gravitational potential is negative.
options.markscheme: This incorrectly suggests that the field strength vectors do not cancel at the point of symmetry for two identical masses.

options.content: The gravitational field strength is non-zero and the gravitational potential is zero.
options.markscheme: This incorrectly treats field strength as a scalar and potential as a vector.

questions.specification: Planet X has a mass $M$ and a radius $R$. Planet Y has a mass $18M$ and a radius $2R$. What is the ratio of the escape velocity from the surface of planet X to the escape velocity from the surface of planet Y?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic D - Fields > D.1 Gravitational fields > D.1.4 Energetics of orbits and escape velocity (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D.1.4 Energetics of orbits and escape velocity (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks for the calculation and comparison of escape velocities for two different planets, which is the primary subject matter of 'Energetics of orbits and escape velocity'.

options.markscheme: This would be the ratio if the escape velocity were directly proportional to the mass and the radius was ignored.

The escape velocity is given by $v_e = \sqrt{ rac{2GM}{R}}$. 
For planet X: $v_X = \sqrt{ rac{2GM}{R}}$. 
For planet Y: $v_Y = \sqrt{ rac{2G(18M)}{2R}} = \sqrt{9 \cdot  rac{2GM}{R}} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{ rac{2GM}{R}} = 3v_X$. 
Therefore, the ratio $v_X : v_Y = 1 : 3$.
This is the ratio of $\frac{M}{R}$ without taking the square root, which is required by the escape velocity formula.
This is the inverse ratio ($v_Y : v_X$).

questions.specification: A planet of mass $6.0 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}$ has a radius of $6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic D - Fields > D.1 Gravitational fields > D.1.1 Laws and properties of gravitational fields
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D.1.1 Laws and properties of gravitational fields
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for gravitational field strength at a planet’s surface using g = GM/r^2, and part (b) uses weight W = mg; both are core properties/laws of gravitational fields.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational field strength at the surface of the planet.
parts.markscheme:
1. Formula: $g=G \frac{M}{r^2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
2. Substitution: $g=\left(6.67 \times 10^{-11}\right) \frac{6.0 \times 10^{24}}{\left(6.4 \times 10^6\right)^2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
3. Simplify: $g=\left(6.67 \times 10^{-11}\right) \frac{6.0 \times 10^{24}}{4.096 \times 10^{13}}$
4. Calculation: $g=9.8 \mathrm{~m\,s^{-2}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To calculate the gravitational field strength, we first identify the values provided in the question:
- Mass of the planet, $M = 6.0 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$
- Radius of the planet, $r = 6.4 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}$

This problem falls under **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**, which deals with the properties of fields around massive bodies.

steps.title: Select the correct formula
steps.content:
From the **Fields** section of your IB Physics data booklet, we find the formula for gravitational field strength at a distance $r$ from a point mass $M$:

$$g = G \frac{M}{r^2}$$

We also need the gravitational constant, $G$, from the **Physical Constants** page:

$$G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^2 \text{ kg}^{-2}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the formula. Ensure you square the radius in the denominator:

$$\begin{aligned} g &= (6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \frac{6.0 \times 10^{24}}{(6.4 \times 10^6)^2} \\ g &= \frac{4.002 \times 10^{14}}{4.096 \times 10^{13}} \\ g &\approx 9.7705... \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (as given in the question), we get $9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression directly, using the exponent key for the radius.

ti84.instructions: Enter the numerator and denominator using parentheses for scientific notation.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula using the standard division slash to create the fraction.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational field strength using the formula.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The gravitational field strength at the surface of the planet is:

$$g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

In an exam, you earn marks for stating the correct formula, showing the substitution, and providing the final answer with the correct units ($\text{m s}^{-2}$ or $\text{N kg}^{-1}$).

steps.title: Understand the equivalence
steps.content: The gravitational field strength, $g$, at the surface of a planet is equivalent to the acceleration a mass would experience due to gravity. We can find this by equating the weight of a test mass $m$, given by $W = mg$, to the universal law of gravitation.

steps.title: Equate the force expressions
steps.content:
We set the weight equal to the gravitational force between the planet (mass $M$) and the test mass $m$ at its surface (distance $r$):

$$mg = \frac{GMm}{r^2}$$

Notice that the test mass $m$ appears on both sides. When we divide both sides by $m$, we get the formula for gravitational field strength found in your data booklet.

steps.title: Identify known values
steps.content:
From the question and the data booklet, we have:
- Mass of the planet ($M$) = $6.0 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$
- Radius of the planet ($r$) = $6.4 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$
- Gravitational constant ($G$) = $6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^2 \text{kg}^{-2}$

The formula we will use is:

$$g = \frac{GM}{r^2}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute these values into the expression. Be very careful with the squared term in the denominator:

$$g = \frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (6.0 \times 10^{24})}{(6.4 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$g = \frac{4.002 \times 10^{14}}{4.096 \times 10^{13}}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational field strength using the formula g = (G * M) / r^2.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Performing the division gives:

$$g \approx 9.7705... \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (as given in the question for the mass and radius), we get:

$$g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

parts.content: Calculate the weight of a $70.0 \mathrm{~kg}$ person on this planet.
parts.markscheme:
1. Using $W=mg$ <marks>M1</marks>
2. Substitution: $W=70.0 \times 9.8=686 \mathrm{~N}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the weight formula
steps.content:
To find the weight of the person, we use the relationship between mass and the acceleration due to gravity. In physics, weight is defined as the gravitational force acting on an object. This is covered in **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**.

The formula for weight from the data booklet is:

$$W = mg$$

steps.title: Substitute the given values
steps.content:
We are given the person's mass and the standard acceleration due to gravity on Earth. 

*   Mass ($m$) = $70.0 \text{ kg}$
*   Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) = $9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$

Substituting these into our equation:

$$W = 70.0 \times 9.8$$

steps.title: Calculate the final weight
steps.content: Now, we perform the multiplication to find the weight in Newtons (N).

gdc.instructions: Multiply 70.0 by 9.8 on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 70.0 * 9.8 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 70.0 * 9.8 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the weight using mass and standard gravity.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculation gives us:

$$W = 686 \text{ N}$$

According to the markscheme, identifying the formula $W=mg$ earns the first mark (**M1**), and the correct substitution leading to $686 \text{ N}$ earns the final mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
To calculate the weight of the person, we need to find the gravitational force exerted on them by the planet. We are given the following information:

*   Mass of the planet ($M$): $6.0 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}$
*   Radius of the planet ($r$): $6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$
*   Mass of the person ($m$): $70.0 \mathrm{~kg}$

steps.title: Select the correct formula
steps.content:
In the **Fields** section of your data booklet, you will find Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation. This formula calculates the force $F$ between two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ separated by a distance $r$:

$$F = G\frac{m_{1}m_{2}}{r^{2}}$$

Here, $G$ is the gravitational constant, which is $6.67 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~N\,m^2\,kg^{-2}}$.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the planetary data and the person's mass into the formula to find the weight:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= (6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times \frac{(6.0 \times 10^{24}) \times (70.0)}{(6.4 \times 10^6)^2} \\ W &\approx 684 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$$

Note: While the markscheme uses $W = mg$ with Earth's standard $g = 9.8 \mathrm{~m\,s^{-2}}$ to get $686 \mathrm{~N}$, calculating it directly from the planet's specific mass and radius provides a more precise result of $684 \mathrm{~N}$ for **this** specific planet.

gdc.instructions: Input the fraction with 6.67e-11 * 6.0e24 * 70.0 in the numerator and (6.4e6)^2 in the denominator.

ti84.instructions: Enter (6.67E-11 * 6.0E24 * 70.0) / (6.4E6)^2 into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the calculator using scientific notation notation (e.g., 10^24).

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational force using mass and radius.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures, the weight of the person on this planet is **$684 \mathrm{~N}$**.

In an IB exam, always look closely at the values provided in the question. If specific mass and radius are given, using the universal gravitation formula is the most rigorous approach to find weight on that specific body.

options.markscheme: This would be the result if the student mistakenly calculated the ratio of $\sqrt{\frac{2}{8}}$ instead of $\sqrt{\frac{8}{2}}$.
This assumes the orbital speed remains constant despite changes to the mass and radius.
The orbital speed is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}}$. For the second satellite, $v' = \sqrt{\frac{G(8M)}{2r}} = \sqrt{4 \frac{GM}{r}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}} = 2v$.
This would be the result if the student forgot to take the square root of the ratio $\frac{8}{2}$.

questions.specification:
The following table provides information regarding two uniform, spherical planets, $X$ and $Y$.

| Planet | Radius | Average Density |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $X$ | $r$ | $\rho$ |
| $Y$ | $3r$ | $2\rho$ |

What is the ratio $\dfrac{g_Y}{g_X}$, where $g$ represents the gravitational field strength at the surface of each planet?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic D - Fields > D.1 Gravitational fields > D.1.1 Laws and properties of gravitational fields
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D.1.1 Laws and properties of gravitational fields
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires calculating the ratio of gravitational field strengths (g) at the surface of two planets using the relationship between gravitational field strength, radius, and density. This falls directly under the laws and properties of gravitational fields.

options.markscheme: This value corresponds only to the ratio of the planets' densities ($2\rho : \rho$).
This value corresponds only to the ratio of the planets' radii ($3r : r$).

The gravitational field strength $g$ at the surface of a spherical planet of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is given by $g = \dfrac{GM}{R^2}$.

Since the planet is a uniform sphere, its mass can be expressed in terms of density $\rho$ and volume $V$:
$M = \rho V = \rho \left( \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \right)$

Substituting this into the formula for $g$:
$g = \dfrac{G \left[ \rho \left( \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \right) \right]}{R^2} = \dfrac{4}{3}G\pi \rho R$

This indicates that for a uniform sphere, $g \propto \rho R$.

For planet $X$: $g_X \propto \rho \cdot r$
For planet $Y$: $g_Y \propto (2\rho) \cdot (3r) = 6\rho r$

Taking the ratio:
$\dfrac{g_Y}{g_X} = \dfrac{6\rho r}{\rho r} = 6$

This distractor might occur if the mass is calculated correctly ($M_Y = 54 M_X$ by cubing the radius) but the student then incorrectly assumes $g \propto \dfrac{M}{R}$ instead of $\dfrac{M}{R^2}$, or if they assume $g \propto \rho R^2$.

parts.content: Define gravitational potential at a point in a gravitational field.
parts.markscheme: - The work done per unit mass in bringing a small test mass from infinity to that point. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the escape speed $v_{esc}$ required for a projectile to leave the surface of Selene-B and reach infinity.
parts.markscheme:
- State energy conservation equation: $\frac{1}{2}mv^2 - \frac{GMm}{R} = 0$ OR $v = \sqrt{\frac{2GM}{R}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Substitute values: $v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.2 \times 10^{22}}{8.5 \times 10^5}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Final value: $1.37 \times 10^3 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ (accept $1.4 \times 10^3 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: A $250 \text{ kg}$ robotic lander is launched from the surface of Selene-B to a parking orbit at a height $h = R$ above the surface. Calculate the change in the gravitational potential energy of the lander.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate potential at surface and at height $R$: $V_1 = -\frac{GM}{R}$, $V_2 = -\frac{GM}{2R}$ 

- Calculate $\Delta E_p = m(V_2 - V_1) = 250 \times (\frac{GM}{2R})$ (or equivalent substitution) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Final value: $1.18 \times 10^8 \text{ J}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 2 marks max if the student uses $mgh$ incorrectly (assuming $g$ is constant).</note>

parts.content: State the relationship between gravitational field strength $g$ and gravitational potential $V$. Explain why gravitational potential values are always negative.
parts.markscheme:
- State relationship: $g = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$ OR field strength is the negative gradient of the potential. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explanation: Potential is zero at infinity; because the gravitational force is attractive, work must be done *by* the field to move a mass towards the body (or work must be done *against* the field to move it to infinity), resulting in a loss of potential energy relative to infinity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A $2000\ \text{kg}$ object is moved from point A at a distance of $1.00 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$ from a planet’s center to point B at $1.60 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$. The planet’s mass is $6.00 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$.

parts.content: Calculate the gravitational potential at points A and B.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate potential at A: $V_A = -\frac{GM}{r} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 6.00 \times 10^{24}}{1.00 \times 10^7} = -4.00 \times 10^7\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate potential at B: $V_B = -\frac{GM}{r} = -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 6.00 \times 10^{24}}{1.60 \times 10^7} = -2.50 \times 10^7\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational potential at a specific distance from a planet, we use the formula for gravitational potential ($V$) for a point mass. This is covered in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**.

$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

Where:
- $G$ is the gravitational constant ($6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{kg}^{-2}$)
- $M$ is the mass of the planet
- $r$ is the distance from the center of the planet

steps.title: Calculate potential at A
steps.content:
First, let's substitute the values for point A. The question states that point A is at a distance of $1.00 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$ and the planet's mass is $6.00 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$.

$$\begin{aligned} V_A &= -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6.00 \times 10^{24}}{1.00 \times 10^7} \\ V_A &= -4.002 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $V_A = -4.00 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.

gdc.instructions: Use the division key to set up the fraction and enter the scientific notation using the 'EE' or '^' key.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation directly into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly using scientific notation.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point A.

steps.title: Calculate potential at B
steps.content:
Next, we repeat the process for point B, using its distance of $1.60 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$.

$$\begin{aligned} V_B &= -\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6.00 \times 10^{24}}{1.60 \times 10^7} \\ V_B &= -2.50125 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $V_B = -2.50 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.

gdc.instructions: Change the distance value in the denominator of your previous expression.

ti84.instructions: Modify the previous calculation by changing the denominator.

desmos.instructions: Update the denominator to the new distance.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point B.

steps.title: State final results
steps.content:
The gravitational potentials at points A and B are:

- $V_A = -4.00 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$
- $V_B = -2.50 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$

Note that potential is always negative (relative to infinity) and becomes less negative (increases) as you move further from the planet.

steps.title: Understand the Potential Energy Approach
steps.content:
To find the gravitational potential ($V$), we first calculate the gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) of the $2000\ \text{kg}$ object. Gravitational potential is defined as the potential energy per unit mass:

$$V = \frac{E_p}{m}$$

This concept is explored in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**. We will use the universal formula for gravitational potential energy from your data booklet.

steps.title: Calculate Energy at Point A
steps.content:
First, let's find the potential energy at distance $r_A = 1.00 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$. We use the mass of the planet $M = 6.00 \times 10^{24}\ \text{kg}$ and the object mass $m = 2000\ \text{kg}$.

$$\begin{aligned} E_{p,A} &= -\frac{GMm}{r_A} \\ E_{p,A} &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \cdot (6.00 \times 10^{24}) \cdot (2000)}{1.00 \times 10^7} \\ E_{p,A} &= -8.004 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the main calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential energy at point A.

steps.title: Determine Potential at Point A
steps.content:
Now, we divide the potential energy by the object's mass ($2000\ \text{kg}$) to find the potential $V_A$:

$$\begin{aligned} V_A &= \frac{E_{p,A}}{m} \\ V_A &= \frac{-8.004 \times 10^{10}}{2000} \\ V_A &= -4.002 \times 10^7\ \text{J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get **$-4.00 \times 10^7\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$**.

gdc.instructions: Divide the previous result by 2000.

ti84.instructions: Divide the previous answer by 2000.

desmos.instructions: Perform the division in a new line.

calculator.description: Divide the calculated energy by the mass of the object.

steps.title: Calculate Energy at Point B
steps.content:
Repeat the process for point B at $r_B = 1.60 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{p,B} &= -\frac{GMm}{r_B} \\ E_{p,B} &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \cdot (6.00 \times 10^{24}) \cdot (2000)}{1.60 \times 10^7} \\ E_{p,B} &= -5.0025 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the energy with the new distance.

ti84.instructions: Modify the previous denominator to 1.60*10^7.

desmos.instructions: Update the denominator in the previous calculation.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential energy at point B.

steps.title: Determine Potential at Point B
steps.content:
Finally, divide the energy at B by the object's mass:

$$\begin{aligned} V_B &= \frac{E_{p,B}}{m} \\ V_B &= \frac{-5.0025 \times 10^{10}}{2000} \\ V_B &= -2.50125 \times 10^7\ \text{J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures gives **$-2.50 \times 10^7\ \text{J kg}^{-1}$**.

gdc.instructions: Divide the result by 2000.

ti84.instructions: Divide the result by 2000.

desmos.instructions: Divide the energy value by 2000.

calculator.description: Divide the energy at B by the mass.

steps.title: Identify the potential formula
steps.content:
To find the gravitational potential ($V$), we use the formula for a point mass. Gravitational potential is defined as the work done per unit mass to move a small test mass from infinity to a point in the field. This concept is covered in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**.

The formula is:
$$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

Where:
- $G$ is the gravitational constant
- $M$ is the mass of the planet
- $r$ is the distance from the center of the planet

steps.title: Calculate potential at point A
steps.content:
First, we calculate the potential at point A ($V_A$) by substituting the given values for the planet's mass and the distance to point A into our formula:

$$\begin{aligned} V_A &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11}) \times (6.00 \times 10^{24})}{1.00 \times 10^7} \\ V_A &= -4.002 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $V_A = -4.00 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point A.

steps.title: Establish the proportional relationship
steps.content:
Since $G$ and $M$ are constants, the gravitational potential is inversely proportional to the distance ($V \propto \frac{1}{r}$).

We can find the potential at point B ($V_B$) by scaling $V_A$ based on the ratio of the distances:

$$\frac{V_B}{V_A} = \frac{r_A}{r_B}$$

This means:
$$V_B = V_A \times \left( \frac{r_A}{r_B} \right)$$

Substituting the distances:
$$\text{Scale Factor} = \frac{1.00 \times 10^7}{1.60 \times 10^7} = 0.625$$

steps.title: Calculate potential at point B
steps.content:
Now, multiply the potential at A by the scale factor to find the potential at B:

$$\begin{aligned} V_B &= (-4.002 \times 10^7) \times 0.625 \\ V_B &= -2.50125 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $V_B = -2.50 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the previous result by 0.625.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous result by (1.00/1.60).

calculator.description: Calculate the potential at B using the scaling factor.

steps.title: Final Answers
steps.content:
The gravitational potentials are:
- At point A: $V_A = -4.00 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$
- At point B: $V_B = -2.50 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$

Note: You earn 1 mark for each correctly calculated potential.

parts.content: Determine the work done in moving the object from A to B.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of work formula: $W = m \Delta V$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitution and answer: $W = 2000 \cdot (-2.50 \times 10^7 - (-4.00 \times 10^7)) = 2000 \cdot 1.50 \times 10^7 = 3.00 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the Energy Formula
steps.content:
To find the work done, we use the fact that work is equal to the change in gravitational potential energy ($W = \Delta E_p$). Since we are moving through a non-uniform gravitational field, we use the formula for gravitational potential energy from **Topic D.1.3**:

$$E_p = -\frac{GMm}{r}$$

Where:
- $G$ is the gravitational constant ($6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{kg}^{-2}$)
- $M$ is the mass of the planet
- $m$ is the mass of the object
- $r$ is the distance from the center of the planet

steps.title: Calculate Energy at Point A
steps.content:
First, we calculate the initial gravitational potential energy ($E_{p,A}$) at point A using the given distance $r_A = 1.00 \times 10^7 \text{ m}$.

$$\begin{aligned} E_{p,A} &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(6.00 \times 10^{24})(2000)}{1.00 \times 10^7} \\ E_{p,A} &= -8.004 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value in the calculation app.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the values into the scientific calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate gravitational potential energy at point A.

steps.title: Calculate Energy at Point B
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the final gravitational potential energy ($E_{p,B}$) at point B using the distance $r_B = 1.60 \times 10^7 \text{ m}$.

$$\begin{aligned} E_{p,B} &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(6.00 \times 10^{24})(2000)}{1.60 \times 10^7} \\ E_{p,B} &= -5.0025 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value in the calculation app.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the values into the scientific calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate gravitational potential energy at point B.

steps.title: Determine Work Done
steps.content:
The work done ($W$) is the difference between the final energy and the initial energy ($W = E_{p,B} - E_{p,A}$).

$$\begin{aligned} W &= (-5.0025 \times 10^{10}) - (-8.004 \times 10^{10}) \\ W &= 3.0015 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:
$$W = 3.00 \times 10^{10} \text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the subtraction.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the result of calculation A from calculation B.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the difference.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in potential energy.

steps.title: Identify the method
steps.content:
To find the work done, we will use the **Gravitational Potential Difference** approach. This involves finding the gravitational potential ($V$) at both positions and then using the relationship between work and potential difference.

The relevant formulas are:
1. Gravitational potential: $$V = -\frac{GM}{r}$$
2. Work done: $$W = m\Delta V$$

steps.title: Calculate potential at A
steps.content:
First, we calculate the gravitational potential at the starting point (point A), located at a distance of $r_A = 1.00 \times 10^7\text{ m}$ from the planet's center. We use the universal gravitational constant $$G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{kg}^{-2}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} V_A &= -\frac{GM}{r_A} \\ V_A &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(6.00 \times 10^{24})}{1.00 \times 10^7} \\ V_A &= -4.00 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point A.

steps.title: Calculate potential at B
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the gravitational potential at the final point (point B), located at a distance of $r_B = 1.60 \times 10^7\text{ m}$.

$$\begin{aligned} V_B &= -\frac{GM}{r_B} \\ V_B &= -\frac{(6.67 \times 10^{-11})(6.00 \times 10^{24})}{1.60 \times 10^7} \\ V_B &= -2.50 \times 10^7 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the gravitational potential at point B.

steps.title: Calculate total work done
steps.content:
The work done ($W$) in moving the mass $m = 2000\text{ kg}$ is the product of the mass and the change in gravitational potential ($\Delta V = V_{\text{final}} - V_{\text{initial}}$).

$$\begin{aligned} W &= m(V_B - V_A) \\ W &= 2000 \cdot (-2.50 \times 10^7 - (-4.00 \times 10^7)) \\ W &= 2000 \cdot (1.50 \times 10^7) \\ W &= 3.00 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earned 1 mark for the formula application and 1 mark for the final correct substitution and answer.

calculator.description: Calculate the final work done.

parts.content: Calculate the average gravitational field strength between A and B using the potential gradient.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate difference in potential and radius: $\Delta V = 1.50 \times 10^7$ and $\Delta r = 0.60 \times 10^7$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate field strength magnitude: $g = \left| -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r} \right| = \frac{1.50 \times 10^7}{0.60 \times 10^7} = 2.50\ \text{N kg}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Explain why the work done is positive even though the field is attractive.
parts.markscheme:
- Work is done against the gravitational field, requiring energy input, which makes the work positive even though the force is attractive. <marks>R1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify displacement direction
steps.content: First, let's look at where the object is going. It starts at $1.00 \times 10^7\text{ m}$ and moves to $1.60 \times 10^7\text{ m}$. Since the distance from the center is increasing, the **displacement is directed away from the planet**.

highlights.text: moved from point A at a distance of 1.00 \times 10^7\text{ m} from a planet’s center to point B at 1.60 \times 10^7\text{ m}

steps.title: Consider the field direction
steps.content: Gravity is an attractive force. This means the planet's gravitational field is pulling the object **inward**, toward the center of the planet. This direction is opposite to the direction we want the object to move.

steps.title: Apply an external force
steps.content: To move the object away from the planet against this inward pull, we must apply an **external force**. This external force must be directed **away from the planet** (outward) to overcome the attractive gravitational force.

steps.title: Link to work formula
steps.content:
In **Topic A.3 (Work, energy and power)**, we learn that work done is calculated using the formula:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

Because the external force we are applying and the displacement of the object are in the same direction (both away from the planet), the angle $\theta$ is $0^\circ$. 

Since $\cos(0^\circ) = 1$, the calculation results in a positive value for work. This represents the energy input required to move the object against the field.

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to degrees. Enter cos(0).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [COS] [0] [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type cos(0) and ensure the setting is in degrees.

calculator.description: Verify that the cosine of 0 degrees is 1 to ensure positive work.

steps.title: Analyze the object's displacement
steps.content: First, let's look at the movement described. The object is moved from Point A ($r = 1.00 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$) to Point B ($r = 1.60 \times 10^7\ \text{m}$). This tells us the object is moving **away** from the center of the planet, increasing its distance from the source of the gravitational field.

highlights.text: moved from point A at a distance of 1.00 × 10^7 m from a planet’s center to point B at 1.60 × 10^7 m

steps.title: Understand gravitational potential energy
steps.content:
In gravitational physics (covered in **Topic D.1: Gravitational fields**), we define the potential energy ($E_p$) as zero at an infinite distance. Because gravity is attractive, $E_p$ is negative at any finite distance. 

As the object moves from Point A to Point B (further away), its potential energy becomes **less negative**. This represents an **increase** in gravitational potential energy.

steps.title: Relate work to energy
steps.content:
Since the gravitational field is attractive, it naturally pulls the object inward. Moving the object outward requires an external force to do work **against** the field. 

Because energy must be supplied to the system to increase its potential energy, the work done on the object is positive. In physics, positive work signifies that energy has been transferred into the system to overcome the attractive force.

highlights.text: Work is done against the gravitational field, requiring energy input, which makes the work positive even though the force is attractive.

questions.specification:
The diagram shows two equipotential surfaces, $X$ and $Y$, in the gravitational field of a planet.

![Equipotential surfaces](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-4b86aa28-8339-45e0-a713-671dc5dcac59-1771929956947/dca59e05-d753-44af-98fc-e6c961f7576c.jpeg)

The gravitational potential at surface $X$ is $-50 \text{ MJ kg}^{-1}$ and the gravitational potential at surface $Y$ is $-20 \text{ MJ kg}^{-1}$. How much work is required to move a $2.0 \text{ kg}$ mass from surface $X$ to surface $Y$?
